tracks.name: Facing Dimensions
tracks.desc: It's maddening how 'underrated' Intracktion is to me. I find it rare to find artists that not only create 'tracks,' but in-depth and profound soundscapes, each with their own accompanying world of vivid audial imagery. Handcrafted complex rhythms accompanied by heart-stained pads and overwhelming space is a weakness of mine. Although being underrated in today's time equates to the lack of followers, I think the handful that do take time to listen to Intracktion's music always experience something intellectually, sonically and aesthetically enlightening.

Classical guitar and piano
A couple of years ago when his school invested in some decent computers and a decent DAW.
He discovered AT a year ago because he got sick of using soundation and put some effort into finding a good online DAW.

Make the music you want to but remember you may find your sound quicker by trying new things and experimenting as much as possible, even if it means listening to new types of music too. By avoiding loops and presets you are forced to create sounds from scratch, not only will this help you understand the synths but it will give you new techniques that can develop into a sound of your own. I have built up a small arsenal of techniques that really define my sound but I'm always adding to it as I'm never completely satisfied. An evolving sound is a healthy sound so always strive to better yourself.

intro: Intracktion, the enigmatic genius. There's hardly another Audiotooler that achieves such a perfect synthesis of melancholy and euphoria in one piece of music. Not least because of his apparently fragile voice, combined with his perfect command of the musical material, a comparison with Radiohead suggests itself.
about:
Kai Franke
Place: Hilden (Germany)
Age: 40
Profession: Musician (self thought)

interview.a: He always had problems to define music.

There was a piano in the family home. His mother remembered how as a small boy he would clamber onto the piano stool, listening to every note with his full concentration. After some months he was playing little tunes with one hand, some months later extemporizing with with two. At the age of eight he started taking piano lessons, but found it boring to learn to play notes. He preferred playing things that came out of himself, not from a paper full of notes. When he was 14 his father bought him a Korg T1. He loved this big keyboard. It had a lot of great synths and drums and a sequencer, so for the first time he could record his compositions on discs. He becam interested in forms of synthesis, creating his own sounds with a Yamaha SY99. With the development of personal computers, recording tools and plug-in technology he did more and more with the computer down to the present time.
At the age of three or four
About two years ago
Let's make some music!

intro: Another Audiotool Chill-Out institution. Nicklas Anderson aka Zone conjures the crystal clear athmosphere of scandinavian nights on our home stereos. Take the time and look who's behind the trademark Zone.
about:
Nicklas Sindlev Andersen
Place: Copenhagen, Denmark. Living in Odense at the moment.
Age: 21
Profession: Student at University of Southern Denmark, studying applied mathematics.

interview.a: Clean melodic Chill-Out

Nicklas took piano lessons at a jazz musician for about 6 years without learning the theory. It was more about playing around and using music as a creative tool to express himself. He learned to play different instruments, but primarily the piano, which he learned to play by ear.
He started creating his own music when he joined audiotool.com. Before that he had played cover songs on the piano.
2011 via Google appstore. Attracted by RNZR's remix of "When stars collide" by Hbeao.

For me, the most important part of making music, is having fun. Audiotool makes this effortless with its intuitive and straightforward layout. With the amazing sample browser at hand and a few simple effects, its easy to make my creativity to flow. Without Audiotool I would probably never have started producing any music or even continued to play an instrument (sorry if this sounds like a bad commercial, but I mean it). For the community i just want to say: Make the music you want to make, don't make the music everyone else wants you to make. Good artists evolve, when they follow their heart, think outside the box and experiment. New sounds and concepts can evolve from simple ideas and this is what makes music such an amazing thing.

Have fun and stay humble. Cheers Zone.

tracks.name: sunshine [edit]
tracks.desc: This is one of my favorite tracks on the Sandorama EP. I literally played this track for days after getting the vinyl. The track seems slightly chaotic while still being in control. When I listen to this track I get the feeling of 'traveling through dimensions'. Evil, wicked catchy and hypnotic vibes, a very well made track.

tracks.name: Program 8
tracks.desc: I absolutely adore Infyuthsion's synth pop tracks, cosmonaut, short circuit etc.. They're so groovy with such a variation in sound and melody, that they're interesting throughout. With this track, Program 8, he really caught the Com Truise spirit in his own way. A very well tailored track with amazing synth bends and stabby bass sounds.

tracks.name: im Kreis tanzen

A minimal track with just the right amount of bass, hi hats and cymbals, the evolving bassline keeps it interesting and gives the track a very refined and wholesome sound. The groove is just perfect, so catchy and what a rhythm.

tracks.name: natural blue

tracks.name: Passive

tracks.name: Technicolour

It's got a light composition, where every element has got its own distinct role: Every sound is perfectly fitting and you can hear every little detail. A well produced and clean track.

tracks.name: Missed
tracks.desc: I had a hard time picking my favorite Aaron track. He's just an amazing artist and he probably makes the most ideal music for me: clean, with the right amount of noise and crisp textures. This is the kind of music I myself strive to make, but fail miserably at. Aaron's latest songs are just astonishingly good and 'Missed' isn't an exception.

tracks.name: what i felt

The glitched mid-range synth changes the mood slightly and adds to the atmosphere. It really makes the track unique and stand out. It's a really well produced track with lots of interesting elements and ideas.

tracks.name: Berlination

tracks.name: Attic - Should I Stay

intro: Talk about Audiotool-Trance-Music and you won't get far without the name Inavon. From the beginning he knew how to use the resources to produce perfect authentic Trance pieces. His "Sole Survivor" remains to this day one of the historical highlights of the website.
about:
Stephen Tai
Place: Fremont, California (USA)
Age: 19
Profession: Studying computer science at University of California, Irvine

interview.a: A lot of uplifting trance, but shifting toward psychedelic and atmospheric styles
He received piano and theory lessons at age 9. Around that time, he also discovered a dance radio station which sparked his interest in trance
2009 with trackers

2009 on the Tony-B Machine, when someone posted a link to Hobnox Audiotool. He didn't know how to use the devices, so he put it aside until 2010 when he created an account. Cripta happened to be a user on the Tony-B Machine so he was a welcome presence on Audiotool

Digital audio workstations (DAWs) exist to assist in the making of music. But that doesn't mean they are easy ways to escape hard work. A DAW is like any other instrument; you must practice in order to gain experience and get better. If you find that your work isn't up to your standards, then familiarity with your tools is more important than more tools. After all, you don't know what that knob does until you twist it, so you should twist it before you get more knobs. So my advice for Audiotoolers is this: if you want to make better music, then eat more peaches.

It was at Audiotool that I started creating my own music
About three years ago. A friend told me about it

Music production has become an important part of my life. Andre Michelle and team, I want to say thank you for this fantastic app! Keep it up!

Dear Audiotoolers,

I would like to quote Zone:

"Make the music you want to make, don't make the music everyone else wants you to make. Good artists evolve when they follow their heart, think outside the box and experiment. New sounds and concepts can evolve from simple ideas and this is what makes music such an amazing thing."

I just want to say:

Have the courage to be yourself. Make music for your heart not for followers.

intro: Martin Below aka mbelow is a powerful force in the Audiotool team. His special field is complex data structures, which he processes with the dynamic drive with which he also approaches drumming in his metal band. A lot of our great innovations are largely due to his unbending will to optimize. Share with us the pleasure of having him on board!
about:
Name: Martin Below
Place: Cologne (Germany)
Age: 34

interview.q: What is your job at Audiotool?
interview.a: I joined audiotool about 1 1/2 years ago and took over Joa's duties on website and backend development. As some of you know, Joa is now focusing on a cross-platform development toolkit called defrac, which we use to build the html5 version of audiotool. Now after the recent relaunch of the audiotool website, I started working with Andre on the App.

interview.q: What is your educational background for the job you are doing?
interview.a: I started studying computer science but dropped it shortly after. Then, I worked as a freelance software developer for different companies in the industry. Oh, and I also studied the drumset, which I think is what qualifies me for my job at audiotool in the first place.

interview.q: When and how did you start programming?
interview.a: I got my first computer in the early nineties (an IBM PC XT with 640KB RAM running at 4.77 MHz). Thank you, mom & dad. At that time, this machine was already outdated and in general not such a good fit for gaming. Well, it could run some simple, mostly text-based games, but all the cool guys in school already had their Amigas and Commodores, which where way ahead in that department. So I started figuring out what else could be done with a computer, and finally looked into this BASIC interpreter thing that was used for running most of these simple games. I think I started just by blindly modifying existing sources and observing the effect of the changes.

interview.q: How would you define your musical style?
interview.a: I have have only made my first few tracks so far, but I think they would fit best in the ambient drawer.
Metal
I started playing drums at the age of 12 and played mostly in thrash- and death metal bands. I was good enough to impress some girls with it, of which one eventually became my wife.

Keep on making this great music on Audiotool! And please be patient. We know that very little has been added to the app in the last months, but there is a lot of stuff going on under the hood. Andre already has a bunch of great ideas for new tools and toys that will be integrated as soon as we have the full-fledged application running on html5. Stay tuned!

tracks.desc: Listening to this covers me in nostalgia. A true classic of emotive garage by Olaf.

tracks.name: Lost (ascent Remix)
tracks.desc: This one here is what sparked me to venture into producing future garage back when I was named Ekwul (very few of you will remember that alias lol). Big big big tune.

tracks.name: 西西里岛 (MCT Drop)
tracks.desc: The perfect balance of heavy and lush, and a track in which I cannot stand still while listening! MCT nailed this beast of a track, and trust me, theres many more from him.

tracks.name: The stars dance for me (sketch)
tracks.desc: Emotion, this piece is made of it. This track spoke to me in ways very few do. An absolutely flawless piece by Jace. For your soul, this is.

tracks.name: being frantic and disillusioned
tracks.desc: enjoyyourshoes, Ladies and Gentlemen. Expression to it's fullest.

tracks.name: Aaron + Nominal - Everything I want is far away
tracks.desc: Step outside your comfort zone, don't be afraid to off-kilter. Deep in the gold mine you go.

intro: Talking about Brighton: When Audiotool Retro Electro is on the agenda you won't get far without Shakey. Since his smash hit "Venus Transit" he has had a firm place among the Audiotool greats with his regular high-quality output. His heroes from the pioneer days of Electro are always there in his sound but they blend in perfectly with the more modern track structures.
about:
Mark Valleley
Place: Brighton, UK
Age: 51 (I know, unbelievable isn't it?)
Profession: Transport Planner however, I always wanted to be a dancer on a cruise liner!

interview.a: Chilled Synth-Pop infused Electro

Played the recorder and sang at infant school. I've had a few guitar lessons over the years but have never been technically proficient enough to be able to express my musicality. Audiotool has changed all that!
When I discovered Audiotool!
I discovered Audiotool as a Chrome Extension about two and a half years ago. I listened to 'Across the Pond' by Olaf and thought "wow, if this is what you can do with this thing, then I'm in!"
I have to come to know, love and meet some talented music makers through Audiotool. I love the fact that I can open up your tracks and steal your ideas!

tracks.name: Across The Pond
tracks.desc: This was one of the first tracks I listened to on Audiotool. It's a haunting soundscape that clearly demonstrated to me the enormous potential of Audiotool as a music making environment. Although Olaf is no longer making music on Audiotool, he has left a great legacy that will continue to inspire others.

tracks.name: helpless
tracks.desc: For me great tracks are those where the whole is greater than the sum of the parts. Tracks that take you to some other place. Like 'Trip 17' by rnzr, this is one of those tracks.

tracks.name: Cosmic Highway
tracks.desc: Pure chill of the highest quality. As it says on the track description, 'Be happy'

tracks.name: Isolation
tracks.desc: Clever of use the Rasselbock by MCT to manipulate two samples and achieve this amazing result. Open it up and see.

tracks.name: Rain Clouds
tracks.desc: Dub and chill all in one. Such an enormous bass on this track! Love the vocal part too!

tracks.name: Empyreal Bound
tracks.desc: The master of electro. No one does it like Chunk!

tracks.name: die form
tracks.desc: Great dark wave track but the question is who is Ascona 400?

tracks.name: suede red
tracks.desc: Like many of the great artists I like on Audiotool, Mr Standfast has carved out his own distinct style. This track would have sat comfortably on Aphex Twin's Selected Ambient Works Vol 1.

tracks.name: Electronic Soul
tracks.desc: This popped up on the site when I was on my way back from Audiotool.Con last October. Listened to it again and again and again on the journey home. Its synth-pop perfection in my opinion. My only criticism would be that it's not long enough!

tracks.name: Find a place right here
tracks.desc: Miami vice style synth-pop from two of the Audiotool greats (sandburgen and cripta). It doesn't get much better than this in my book!

tracks.name: Chasing Happiness
tracks.desc: Artist deserving more attention. Jordi has clearly been immersed in music and other creative activities for many years and this clearly shows in his rich, layered compositions full of lush sounding synths.

intro: Liam Robertson aka opaqity is not only the youngest in the round of our featured artists: he has also managed in the shortest time to teach himself how to produce electronic music and, with his relaxed chill-out minimal tracks, has become one of the currently most frequented Audiotoolers. Find out for yourselves.
about:
Name: Liam Robertson
Place: Glasgow, Scotland (UK) "I lived in Minnesota, USA until moving to the UK at age nine"
Age: 13
Profession: Williamwood High School

interview.a: I don't really like to limit myself to a particular style or genre, but what I make is usually pretty chilled out. Other than that, I don't know what I'd call it. It's often focused on synths, chords and melodies, but sometimes I go through future garage stages where percussion is the main thing.

I've always been into piano. Even before I started getting lessons at the age of six, I'd play around, making up little pieces. I've always preferred composing small songs on the piano over playing pieces by other people, and piano is still a major part in making my tracks.
Although I'd been making music on the piano many years before, I only started making music electronically earlier this year.

I actually found audiotool well over a year ago in search of an easy way to make music. This was when I really started to get into lots of different types of music - and I really wanted to try it out for myself. I gave up after a week. Everything I tried to do just ended up turning into trash, and so I forgot about audiotool for a long time, until about half a year ago - something brought me back to the site, and some of the stuff I was hearing was really impressive. I decided to give it another shot, and for the past 5 months I've been hooked.

Audiotool is the coolest way to get into making music. The relatively small community makes everyone's presence so much more valuable - so make the music you love. Audiotool is where you can get away with that. By making the music you want to hear, you will end up with a group of people who love your music, which is what we all need as artists starting out on Audiotool.

tracks.name: Tag 10 von zehn
tracks.desc: Sandburgen really manages to create such an engaging atmosphere in his tracks, and this is definitely one that stuck in my mind

tracks.name: where we fell in
tracks.desc: Jace is one of the most inspirational artists here for me, and he has such an amazing ability to get emotion across in his tracks. The percussion is phenomenal

tracks.name: all good things...
tracks.desc: Some insane talent on display here. I've lost count of how many times I've played this, just closing my eyes and listening

tracks.name: Airglow
tracks.desc: This one has a great pace, while still managing to stay wonderfully chill. A fantastic, uplifting track from an Audiotool great

tracks.name: Coma
tracks.desc: Mikke smashed it with this stunning soundscape. Spacey vibes coming from this one

tracks.name: Peach
tracks.desc: I've always been a fan of VLTRA's sound since I first heard his stuff. This track really submerges you in a wonderful atmosphere

tracks.name: Watashi Zone
tracks.desc: I love everything about this. The slow, crackly percussion over the melodies is absolutely mesmerising

tracks.name: thesis
tracks.desc: I admire the way Mr. Standfast is able to make such great sounds while keeping it so simple. I love these melodies

tracks.name: Freedom
tracks.desc: I really can't get over Pathfinder's stuff. The 80's vibes are incredible - this is totally different for Audiotool

tracks.name: Before you became an Angel
tracks.desc: This is immensely satisfying stuff. The whole thing is just so dreamy.

tracks.name: Time Machine
tracks.desc: Recommended artist. Pathfinder produces some really crazy stunning stuff. I particularly love the 80's tracks. It makes me sad to see it only get a few listens. Go check out his work!

tracks.name: Guilt
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. This isn't the best example of the kind of thing I make most, but I really like the energy and pace in this.

intro: From the early days of Audiotool White Grizzly has been considered to guarantee dismal sound-constructions. He is not restricted to crude eerie effects, but looks deep into the human subconscious and manages to express even the most hidden, basest emotions in his music.
about:
Name: Massimo Buffetti
Place: Genoa (Italy)
Age: 48
Profession: Civil and Industrial Automation Installer

interview.a: My style - I do not know how to define it. I really like Ambient in all variations: Dark-, Electronic, Relaxation. I really like to experiment with different sounds.
I have been making music since I was 14 years.

First with an old synth of "Bontempi" that I disassembled and modified several times to create different sounds. Then I built various mechanical and electronic synths. Then moved on to a "Motif" Yamaha. But this no in my possession for economic reasons.
I discovered Audiotool four years ago when looking for online music programs, and I am delighted to have found it.

I sincerely thank you for this great opportunity you give to unknown artists to let the world hear their creative output and share their musical experience. There are many good musicians unknown and it is fantastic to get to know them on Audiotool.

tracks.name: Earth Machine
tracks.desc: Mikke dedicated this track to me, as someone who is not able to love. Fantastic like all his Ambient tracks.

tracks.name: Last Train to Oblivion
tracks.desc: Hiro Avalon has a creative imagination that I appreciate very much. This track is beautiful in its simplicity.

tracks.name: DS-Calling Yog-Sothoth
tracks.desc: Potorato has always fascinated me with his creations. This track is brilliant.

tracks.name: Acid
tracks.desc: LowFeel is never enthusiastic about his creations. Like a true artist, he does not understand his enormous creative quality.

tracks.name: blumendrone
tracks.desc: Noraus is unique on Audiotool. This one makes my mind fly to distant worlds. Pity it is so short.

tracks.name: J.C
tracks.desc: When Cripta decides to create Ambient he is unrivaled. With simple, traditional sounds he makes my fantasy travel in forgotten areas of the mind.

tracks.name: Death
tracks.desc: Karolina Kalinowsca has a brilliant mind. I've always loved his creations. You can hear the subtle, hidden contempt for society in his music.

tracks.name: Nomad, same [Day 8]
tracks.desc: I came across this set of tracks when just relaxing. Often simplicity is little appreciated.

tracks.name: Fading Memory
tracks.desc: This track of Versus has inspired me a lot. With its sounds I created a lot of tracks.

tracks.name: rain
tracks.desc: Sumad introduced me to Dub Techno. This track is really great. I envy his creativity.

intro: Patrick Kuessner aka TRiCK stands for atmospheric Minimal Techno with no fancy stuff. But it's particularly his trips into the field of Dub-Techno that have made him an Audiotool institution.
about:
Name: Patrick Kuessner
Place: Mainz (Germany)
Age: 31

interview.a: Minimalistic Dance Music
When I was in kindergarden my brother had a Yamaha DX7 and I loved making helicopter noises on that thing. Later I had lessons with a keyboard and a clarinette, but I did not really like it.
I started making my own music in 2009 when I found the audiotool beta on hobnox.
I was reading an article about the roland TR 808 and googled for an emulation. And so I found Audiotool.
Audiotool forever

tracks.name: droid
tracks.desc: Good times. I could listen to Olondro for hours these days.

tracks.name: Le Kraft
tracks.desc: Le is French for the and Kraft the german word for power and damn this has got style.

tracks.name: Corner Shop
tracks.desc: A 6-step sequence, unbelievable. robert hood did that before, as I have meanwhile learned. 3,4 and 5 steps is cool too, so I found out.

tracks.name: Brigidino Boogie
tracks.desc: Skills - does that come from a professional studio or what?

tracks.name: Mr Messy(Raw)
tracks.desc: One of the newer techno artists on audiotool that I really like.

tracks.name: distant moons
tracks.desc: Epic and legendary, I deeply love this.

tracks.name: In Detroit
tracks.desc: Techno from Brasil inspired by the motorcity, where it all began.

tracks.name: Love & Fail (sumad remix)
tracks.desc: That's what I call some good old Techno

tracks.name: Audiotea
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. Good spirit, fun track.

intro: It's a pleasure to follow In5omniac's career on Audiotool, the progress he has made since the early days is unmistakeable. While at the beginning it was still raw hackneyed phrases in which the musical background could only just be made out, today it has ripened into Minimal-Techno and Deep-House pieces that would have a good chance on the music market. An Audiotool musician who would have deserved more attention.
about:
Name: Marian Sdraila
Place: Cluj-Napoca (Romania)
Age: 28
Profession: Architecture student at Technical University of Cluj-Napoca

interview.a: Every track is a new beginning meaning that I never know what I will end up doing. But my favorite genres will always be Minimal Techno and Deep House
Although I always felt an attraction towards music I never had any training as a musician.
About 4 or 5 years ago.
Since the beginning of audiotool. A friend told me about it.

I started using Ableton not so long after I discovered audiotool and I have to say that learning Ableton would have been a lot harder without audiotool. It's the perfect platform for anyone who would like to learn about making electronic music and also about a little piece of electronic music history, because of the great classical machines emulated in it. What I will like to add is that, although I am an Ableton user, I always come back to audiotool, first of all for its accessibility and second for the great community that it has.

tracks.name: Analog Jelly
tracks.desc: This track was for me love at first hearing! The moment I first listened to it I knew that this is one of the tracks that I would want to come back to, so that's how this playlist started.

tracks.name: droplets ft amoeba
tracks.desc: This is the kind of track that makes you forget about everything for a few moments. It takes you into another dimension.

tracks.name: Unhappy tree friends
tracks.desc: I think there is no need for an introduction to this or any other track made by Sndbrgn. He has the greatest melodies in his tracks.

tracks.name: Off-.
tracks.desc: The greatest soundscape I have ever came across in audiotool.

tracks.name: table tennis
tracks.desc: I don't even know where to start.....great percussions, sublime synthwork and a simplicity that I always loved on a track.

tracks.name: WEDDNG
tracks.desc: Is that supposed to be 'wedding'? Anyhow, I'm looking forward to the days when this kind of music will be played at all the weddings.

tracks.name: Night Rain
tracks.desc: Man, this guy should be a superstar by now with this kind of track! Sublime work.

tracks.name: KTN-11
tracks.desc: I saved a spot for one of かたな's tracks, but it was a really hard choice because there are tons more that are really great techno tracks. You should check them out if you haven't already.

tracks.name: Influence
tracks.desc: This is a classic, for me at least, but I have to say that this is one of many great tracks that Marodeur has produced over the years. And I think it's safe to say that he is by far the best minimal-techno artist on audiotool.

tracks.name: 3:14 AM
tracks.desc: I just discovered this one recently and I think it's perfect for my playlist ending. Awesome percussions and a really great atmosphere.

tracks.name: Heh
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. He's got some really great techno tracks - certainly deserves more attention.

tracks.name: Sleeplessness
tracks.desc: My best track. This is one of my first tracks and also one of the first tracks that has been featured on audiotool. It might not be the best track from the sound quality point of view but it has something that my other tracks are lacking (more or less), and that is emotion!

intro: With his authentic Retro style Jordi Moragues has secured himself a firm place among the popular Audiotool artists. Crystal clear synth melodies reminiscent of forerunners of electronic music like Vangelis and Jean Michel Jarre. Check him out and feel the spirit of those pioneer days.
about:
Name: Jordi Moragues Massanet
Place: Barcelona, Spain. Currently I live in Cologne, Germany
Age: 44
Profession: Currently dance teacher. Before that I was a computer animator. Studied and worked at the University of the Balearic Islands and the Academy of Media Arts in Cologne.

interview.a: Classic, or Retro, highly influenced by the popularization era of electronic music in the late 70s and early 80s and artists like Vangelis, Jarre or Kraftwerk.

I don't have formal training, have always been self-taught, but recently I have started to study and educate myself in music theory on my own. I also don't have any professional experience, apart from making the soundtrack of most of my old computer animation shorts. Making music has always been a hobby for me.
In the early 90s at home, with a Roland W-30, to which later I added a few more instruments from Roland and Korg.

Just by chance when it was still part of Hobnox, some time around 2008, looking for music-making online programs. I was curious but did not stick to it. I rediscovered it at the end of 2012 as Audiotool.com and have been hooked to it ever since.

Thanks a lot to Andre and his team for this fantastic platform. I am constantly learning with it and don't think I will ever feel limited. I am looking forward to their new ideas for the upcoming version. I am also amazed at the talent and the high quality of the music I have found in this community. I encourage audiotoolers to keep creating and experimenting. Research and listen to lots of music, learn the basics of music theory, copy and understand the techniques from the pros, open and analyse tracks you like to understand what makes them tick, experiment a lot, know each device inside out, get creative, try unusual arrangements and don't feel pressed to publish too often.

tracks.name: Server drops
tracks.desc: One of my first finds in Audiotool. He's an expert at creating cool, chill and absorbing landscapes punctuated by cheeky bass-lines.

tracks.name: Cubrick
tracks.desc: Any Audiotool inspiration list should include Intracktion. He occupies a category of his own. Outstanding compositions, arrangements, sound design, structure, emotion and even vocal skills. I could have picked any track of his.

tracks.name: EXPERIMENT
tracks.desc: I love hypnotic ostinatos and this track effectively uses them together with very expressive sound effects and an amazing bass-line. A track to get lost in.

tracks.name: Bondage "Act 2"
tracks.desc: This could be a great soundtrack for a psycho-thriller. The sound atmosphere is full of ghosts lurking in the background, claustrophobic, disturbing yet fascinating.

tracks.name: Obscured Shadows
tracks.desc: This guy makes the best Electro I have found in Audiotool, groovy and cheerful. As another user put it: 'clean, wobbly, retro, electro, synthy, paddy, bassey'. Kraftwerk with a sweet sense of humour.

tracks.name: yemen
tracks.desc: Great Techno track. This is how it's done in my opinion; heavy, obsessive and naughty. The sound design is great, full of subtle details. Reminiscent of Andrea Parker.

tracks.name: 17nm
tracks.desc: The king of sound design. His pads, basses, arps, effects and resulting atmospheres have no equal. Open his tracks for a master-class in electronica production. I wish he published more often.

tracks.name: horen y dwr
tracks.desc: Whoever this is, they don't seem to be publishing any more but I wish they would. The music is outstanding, experimental in a FSOL kind of way, very visual and well produced.

tracks.name: Long Long Winter Night
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. He published his first and to date only track just a week ago but its refined quality suggests great things to come. Watch him.

tracks.name: Chasing Happiness
tracks.desc: Favourite own track. My latest work. I feel that the quality of this track has benefited from everything I have learned so far making the previous ones.

intro: To reduce Nionsomnia to one style would be a sin. Over and over again he has demonstrated his command of Dubstep, Hip Hop - including singing his own Raps - and other variations of Bass Music. But he also produces heart-rending Trance synths and even banging Hardstyle Beats with an authenticity of style that would suggest a much more experienced producer than he can be at his tender age.
about:
Name: Nionwa Jahi (Nee-ahn-wa) (Ya-hee)
Place: Riverside, California (USA)
Age: 17
Profession: Summerville Highschool. I'm studying electricity.

interview.a: I make all types of music as long as it displays feeling or a good message. I honestly just make whatever mood I'm in at the moment. Any song I make comes from what the times have called for. What the movement of spirit has called for. So I would define my style as... movement. And feeling. I hardly ever know what genre I'm going to end up with at the end of the day.

My whole family has always been artistic in some way. Whether it be speaking, writing, cooking, martial arts or playing an instrument. I've always been surrounded by instruments and have always banged on drums, but took it seriously when I got my drum set at 10 years old. Everyday down in the basement I just devoted my life to playing music, and progressively, I got better and better. It was never something that I planned out. It was also never really something I got lessons for. It's not like I was against lessons, but looking back I guess I was self-assured so that whatever the preferred sound it came from my head, onto the drums, and out to the universe and was meant to sound like that. If I had taken lessons it would have just sounded like the preferred sound in the instructor's head that they could say was proper, but not genuine for me. From the drums came guitars, and keyboards, and little cheap radios that had cassettes in them that my brother and I would attempt to record professional-sounding rock songs on (yeah right). But at the time it was golden, because the theme of it was creation. And creation is life. From then my love for creating grew and in my freshmen year in high school my friend introduced me to audiotool. For a while I would just listen to all the artists on there while doing school work, just wondering how in the world they made all those amazing sounds. One day, I clicked the "APP" button and literally just started creating. Knowing real instruments I guess it was a plus when I saw all the guitar looking pedals that I later found were plugins for effects. And from there, I've just been learning more and more about electronic music, or even making music in general.
I started making music at 10.
My friend showed me the site. He said he didn't really know how to use it and that I should check it out. So I did.

I always felt love for all the artists on here that devote their time to doing what they love the most, which is making music. And the ones who are into vibing with the times and are open to a positive change. On your journey, have fun and learn from people, but be your own biggest critic. Don't get hung up so much on what someone else has to say, because then you are no longer going off the music that comes from your soul, but the sound that someone else will forget about the next day. Embrace self and things will flow. Because what matters more: the name, or the character behind the name?

tracks.name: Dusk to Dawn
tracks.desc: The first real garage song I heard anywhere on audiotool. Audiotool is actually where I learned that the name for this is 'Garage'. The mysterious kind of intro and the spooky sounding pads that fade kind of in snippets really make the song. The drums are also flawless and at the time I first heard it I was really into playing drums. So I spent like 5 hours that day trying to imitate the electronic drum patterns from the song on my real drums. That was of course back then, when I hadn't learned about sequenced drums versus real drums. I still get goose bumps listening to it.

tracks.name: Phoenix ft. Aaron - Trying to be cool
tracks.desc: I came across this when I was really into a lot of electronic music that had 140 bpm kind of rap influence using samples; artists like XXYYXX or Daedalus. I also love the band Phoenix. So when I heard this I kind of lost it. The song's still on my phone actually.

tracks.name: Dirty Dan (Dubstep)
tracks.desc: Some dirty dirty dub. Enough said. The creepy feeling the beginning has that leads up to the drop is so sudden that it intensifies everything. This is what got me making scary sounding dub for a while.

tracks.name: Soundwaves
tracks.desc: Almate and I kind of rose up together. I'm happy he's improved so much. This song actually got me into experimenting with dnb production. My first attempt was when I remixed Soundwaves. It was a pretty big success for me.

tracks.name: Chill Penguins
tracks.desc: This is a really glitchy track in a good way. Abstract always seems to maintain a pop in his songs. This inspired me to write Time Bomb, which made top 100 in Moogfest.

tracks.name: NXS#_004
tracks.desc: When I heard a few of Solace's hits, it really made me question my dnb skills in general. He's got drum and bass on lock. After a while I started getting ideas from him and ended up getting better at making dnb.

tracks.name: Make Me Feel
tracks.desc: I have a deep appreciation for classic house. The 80s-sounding sample really tied this song together. This proved to me that any genre is possible on Audiotool.

tracks.name: Cherry Blossom Road
tracks.desc: It's all in the strings in this song. The violins set the mood for the track and the percussion somehow fits right in with it. Offbeat's usage of chopped samples in instrumental hip hop really reminds me of nujabes and has inspired me to try more of that type of stuff. Now I can never get enough of digging through records.

tracks.name: Meteor Flare
tracks.desc: In my opinion one of the very best constructed and best sounding dubstep songs on Audiotool. This inspired me to write Pyrostepping.

tracks.name: Is this The World?
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation: Udsinep. An underrated artist on here in my opinion.

intro: Cripta aka Marco Falcini is the Audiotool Techno producer of the first hour and creator of the legendary quote "No Pulve no party". Hard to believe that he brought with him no previous skills in music production. Always stylistically confident, always authentic he delivers one dance-floor filler after another.
about:
Name: Marco Falcini
Place: Livorno (Tuscany, Italy)
Age: 35
Profession: Currently unemployed (thanks Italy, love you so much) what's school ?? :)

interview.a:
It depends on the inspiration of the moment. But yes, most of my tracks are Techno-house and derivates.

If i still had a guitar and a bass, I would definitely make more Rock 'n Roll :)
Piano, bass, guitar, drums, then DJing and producing. Everything autodidact and just for fun. That's why I suck at them all ;)
I started messing around with electronic music softwares 5-6 years ago, with Ableton and Reason, then Audiotool.

I was looking for an online music maker program and found Audiotool browsing the internet.

It was still called Hobnox. But I never used it because I couldn't. I published my first track when it had already entered the Audiotool era, after some months testing and studying the software.

For audiotoolers: NO PULVE NO PARTY (of course)

For the staff: well, you are all great people, and the site is great. Wish I was a software programmer born in Cologne, so I could help you in some way. And can't wait for the new shit.

tracks.name: Mother (preview)
tracks.desc: Dark bangin Techno at its best: powerful, angry, hypnotic... SPEECHLESS!!!

tracks.name: Heisen-Fest
tracks.desc: Sandburgen is a genre. He has his unique, proven, and recognizable sound. Ranging from Techno to Trance, via New Wave, Post Punk influences! I challenge you to press play and to not dance... KILLER RIFF !!!

tracks.name: path II
tracks.desc: Olondro has his definite and recognizable style, always original and never obvious or boring. I love a lot of Olondro tracks, it was really hard to choose only one. But probably this is my favourite.... TRICKY !!

tracks.name: Cubrick
tracks.desc: He came on Audiotool and boom! A new world of sound! GENIUS!!!

tracks.name: Abducted
tracks.desc: Probably the AT artist closest to my taste. His music is a sexy underground club. It was hard too choose only one track cause I love them all. But this is maybe my favourite. 'how to get high without drugs' ... ECSTASY!!!

tracks.name: Soundlab
tracks.desc: He's another one of my greatest inspirations. I could not believe his tracks were made on Audiotool, so professional. I learnt everything opening and spying his tracks. And this track is one of my favourites of his. But eh... missing any of Chunk tracks is a crime. MASTER OF SYNTHESIS!!

tracks.name: Pointless Tune
tracks.desc: EliaTrix is another pioneer of the 'audiotool techno club'... His tracks are always dark, mindblowing and tripping... WASHING BRAIN MACHINE!!!

tracks.name: toys are tools too!
tracks.desc: Yes, he was right, toys rule ;) Thanks for this track Thomas - it is probably the reason why I'm on audiotool.... EVERGREEN!!

tracks.name: Corner Shop
tracks.desc: Open this track. You won't believe your eyes. Ascent is simply a fantastic producer with very good taste, too. What else ? For me a point of reference... TOP !!!

tracks.name: Find a place right here
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. Even if it's not perfect as regards production, I love the composition and the sounds. It's easy but engaging and perfectly focused on our initial target.

tracks.name: Blinded
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. Because he's really young and so talented! I've rarely seen people get his skills, in such a short time, and starting from zero experience. Keep it up bro!

intro: With JamBam and Retro you're not talking about bombastic Synthpop ballads or tempestuous cow-bells. Without a doubt his style was shaped by the club sounds of the early 1990s. Be it Acid, Trance or the raw Drum and Bass sounds of the end of the century, he's got them all at his fingertips and has only to add that distinctive JamBam something. His track "Set phazers on everything" made it onto the AT Cassette for the Moogfest 2014 from more than 1,000 applicants.
about:
Name: Jamie Cooling
Place: The U.K. originally form Canterbury in Kent but I've lived in London long enough to have a cockney accent :)
Age: 26, definitely 26.............. I am absolutely not any older than 26........... ok I'm 35
Profession: Alcoholic beverage intake management consultant (barman)

interview.a: I have no idea what I'm going to make when I open the app but I would have to say I'm predominantly about acid. I love everything 303, seriously if I was from Texas I probably would have married one by now. I dabble in ambient, trance and housey stuff too and I've developed a real love of making dnb. At the moment I've been listening to a lot of hard glitched up stuff like square pusher and there's lots of crazy stuff coming out of Japan (isn't there always?). It's tough to do and takes a long time but I reckon it's a direction I would like to go in, so long as I can fit a 303 in it somewhere!

I haven't the foggiest on how to play anything! I did used to carry around a guitar but that was just to get girls. In the 90's my older brother was in a DJ collective called 'Cloud Nine'. Through them I got into the clubbing and free party scene and I quickly developed a love for everything about it. The gigs, the atmosphere, the people you met and all this amazing new music like acid, techno, trance, hardcore, dnb etc. Then working in bars pubs and clubs I was always surrounded by music and I always wanted to try my hand at producing. But on a barmans wage there was no way I could afford all the gear needed. Then I found Audiotool...
On Audiotool, but if you count air guitaring to Nirvana - 1993

About three years ago I think, I was messing about on StumbleUpon and Andre's Tonematrix popped up. Before I knew it I'd been sat there for about half an hour playing around, I noticed the banner saying "Check out my day job" clicked on it and there it was, this amazing site full of great music and cool people!

My mind gets blown by the music on Audiotool everyday. Keep doing it! This site is just brilliant, put together with dedication and passion by the devs and populated by an awesome (if slightly disfunctional) family ;) It means someone like me (a guy that didn't even know what a sine wave was) can make music and even get featured. Oh and on that note - YEAH BITCHES I GOT FEATURED! HAHA!!!!!!! I'M OFF TO MARRY A KARDASHIAN! THE WORLD NEEDS MORE JAMBAM!

tracks.name: Warm Greeting (Nominal Cold Farewell Remix)
tracks.desc: When I first discovered Audiotool I mainly just listened to a lot tracks while staring at the app in total confusion. Then this track was featured, within about ten minutes I was in the app determined to make music.

tracks.name: destroyed me
tracks.desc: If Nom got me started it was Torn that got me going, the help and encouragement was invaluable and it made me stay on AT. This track is definitely my fave of his - that robot voice, the breaks with the melancholy pads, beautiful. Makes me want to buy the guy a pint and listen to his story.

tracks.name: REmix II (Ksvbi)
tracks.desc: Ah Tott, you know the guy is so chilled and laid back I'm pretty sure he's a rastafarian, and that mellow attitude is reflected in his music. Hehe na he is the master of dark dnb and to me this is his best, that grimey bass hitting you in waves with the amen on top, class! Plus this is the guy that got me into making dnb, cheers dude you're a legend!

tracks.name: ...and then all this crazy-ass robots appeared
tracks.desc: I have this as my ringtone :) I opened this up had a look and learned tons, so simple and yet the sound design is so good, the beats, those little blips, that base and that title..... totally weird, totally brilliant.

tracks.name: A Certain Kind of Bullet
tracks.desc: And now we're getting into the 303 side of things! This is the kind of music I love in clubs; hard, melodic and with a gritty acid line going through it.

tracks.name: The Simpleton (2014)
tracks.desc: A seriously talented dude who was a massive help early on in AT, he even took the time to make me a template explaining compression, truely 'la creme de la fromage' ;) And this track, dark, heavy, almost suffocating 303 with that muffled thudding kick.......it's a panic attack in a track, love it.

tracks.name: Jalousie
tracks.desc: I wish I could make music like this, my god he's good at doing glitched up percs and beats! And when the vocals come in..... yeah. This guy deserves way more faves than he gets, I literally have an album of his stuff on itunes that'll take me a day to listen to, get on his page and get listening!

Again if I'm making a list Inavon has got to be in it. In my opinion he's the best at making trance on AT, his arps and pads are always spot on and then mixing in the 303 - perfect, I'd be very happy bouncing along to this on a beach in Goa!

tracks.name: Broken Projector
tracks.desc: This has got to be in the selection, I loved that almost guitar sound so much I started experimenting with the slope on a high res over synths. I then went on to make two tracks using the stuff I came up with, genuinely inspirational. Also come on it's Kepz, need I say more?
Underrated Artist. With an excellent mix of house, electro and trancy elements SSagara really deserves more attention. This track is so catchy it was stuck in my head for days, I had to download it!

tracks.name: Set Phazers to..... Everything
tracks.desc: My best track. This is the track that got into Moogfest, thank you again guys! It was a labour of love and hate, the notes, pad, bass and progression all fell right into place. The vocoded vocals drove me mad! But I think they turned out ok.

tracks.name: The Acid Truth
tracks.desc: And just cos I'm a cheeky sod I'm going to try and sly another one in for the sheer love of 303! :)

intro: Whole-hearted musician and number three of the most popular Audiotool artists. Patrick White aka Uprising comes up with professional sounding Bass-Music of all categories and thus serves as a model for many of our up and coming producers. It's only his output rate lately that gives any cause for complaint. Come on, Up!
about:
Name: Patrick White
Place: Tulsa Oklahoma (USA)
Age: 38
Profession: Sous-chef in a fine

interview.a: I like to make DnB, Dubstep, Hip hop, Chill, Classical and Experimental. I also like to try to make pretty much anything I think would be fun to do.

I original started my journey into music when I was about 8 years old with the violin. I played violin all the way through school and still play whenever I get a chance.

After school I played Guitar in a Death Metal band called Deadfall. We had lots and lots of fun. After Deadfall, I played bass in a band called Plexity in Houston TX. We had some mild successes and a lot of fun.

I didn't start making music on the Computer until I discovered Audiotool.

I knew I wanted to do something with the computer and music so I searched for apps that I could play with for fun and quickly found Audiotool.

At first, I thought Audio tool was a neat little toy so played around with sampling and the beat machines and published it.

This is when I found out that this new toy of mine had a whole community attached to it.

I remember it well. It was Tornsage who first commented on and faved my track. Then he followed me. This made me feel real good, so I did what any respectful new Audiotooler would do and spammed my crappy little sampled track all over creation and back. This is when I got the best advice of my life on AT. It was left in a comment by a few different people. It read something like this, "Stop spamming your tracks all over the place and learn to use the Pulverisateur. If you do this and participate in the community people will find you!"

I took this advice and started listening to tracks and I quickly found that Audiotool can do so much more then I had originally thought. I started to love the music I was hearing and talking to people who were way more active than I ever anticipated people being on a little music toy that I found in the google app store.

This is When Audiotool Stopped being a just toy for me and became a way of life and a place to hang and make music with people I respect and care about. I am now hooked for life.

You guys are amazing. It is a pleasure be a part of all the talent and musical originality that is Audiotool. The support we have for each other cannot be found on any other website in the universe and for that I thank you. Audiotool is where all the cool kids hang and I am glad to be a part of it.

tracks.name: Chill Penguins
tracks.desc: When I heard this track is when I first really started becoming a fan of Abstract and his funky style. Easily one of my favorite tracks ever on AT.

tracks.name: tightrope
tracks.desc: The one and only great, Tornsage! Need I say more.

tracks.name: Dubcandy
tracks.desc: Another one of my favorite tracks of Audiotool. What really got me with this track were those magical pads mixed with those nasty noisy basses and a mean ass pluck to give it that 'Fuck yeah' feel.

tracks.name: Resonance Sector
tracks.desc: Cgman is f**king insane. Nuff said

tracks.name: Wandering In Mist
tracks.desc: Inavon has long been one of my favorite artists of Audiotool. I like this track because it is beautifully written. I like how Inavon pays attention to the details. All those little things add up to make great music for us to listen to. This track a fantastic example of how the people of Audiotool can make music using Audiotool that can hold up to today's standards in most genre.

tracks.name: I'm a Potato 2
tracks.desc: Bluedude, Probably one of the most underrated Artists on Audiotool. This track has great sound design and style.

tracks.name: Downhill
tracks.desc: When I first heard this song I was going through some tough but good changes in my life. This is the first song on audiotool that gave me real Goosebumps and made me feel Good inside. I only wish I could write music this good.

tracks.name: Watashi Zone
tracks.desc: A great track by zone. Some real Chill sh*t. I like this because it sounds great to me in every way.

tracks.name: Boogie-Moogie
tracks.desc: Frigolito has more raw talent than any of us. He has an understanding of music and the instruments used to create it that only few I have ever met have. Frig is a f**king audio prodigy.

tracks.name: Cripta-ology!:)
tracks.desc: This is one of my favorites from Yafee? They guy whose picture looks like a colonoscopy. MOVEMBER! Get checked every one!

tracks.name: OBJeCT
tracks.desc: Shout out to Potasmic as an artist. This track just grabs me from the beginning with its unique sound design and original style and holds me down to the very last note.

tracks.name: Dirty Dan (Dubstep)
tracks.desc: My best track. I love the horror genre and I think that this track is very unique to Audiotool. I love the story I wrote for it and think I did a good job of creating the actual villain in the story using the crusher.

intro: For some time we can observe a steady increase in Trap producers and tracks. One of the driving forces behind this development is B-ST from Senegal. Never denying his Hip Hop roots, he produces one mature Trap beat after another. Definitely worth checking out!
about:
Name: Bocar Talla
Place: Senegal, but currently live in the US
Age: 17
Profession: Music Producer
School: Southwick-Tolland-Granville Regional High School (S.T.G.R.H.S)

interview.a: Especially with my latest beats, I would say that my style is somewhat cinematic yet can very easily be described as Hip Hop or Trap depending the genre I'm going for. I usually just go with what I feel like works the best when making a track (but obviously with some foundation).

From a younger age, music has always been in my heart. As a kid, I would always tap to the beat of any song I heard. My brother used to rap and I would always watch him and his friends playing with FL Studio and recording. My dad sings and plays the guitar, keyboard, and drums. When I was 11, my dad (who sings and plays guitar, keyboard and drums) taught me the c scale and from them on I tough myself how to play keyboard/piano from videos on YouTube.

Around age 11-12 the online game Fantasy DJ wad what started me up in music production. The first program I used was Soundation, then I moved to ClubCreate, and later found Audiotool; now I produce beats using FL Studio as well.

At the time when ClubCreate was gradually fading from the internet, I started to search for other free web-based studios I could make music on, and so I found Audiotool; which I immediately left because it was so foreign to me and I had no idea how it worked. A month later I came back and stepped into a whole new world and embarked on the journey that brought me where I am now.

To the people just starting Audiotool or making music itself, making music may not come easily to all of us, but that's no reason why we can't be as good as we want to be. There's nothing wrong with sampling; some of the best music out there has samples. If you're just starting out, don't be afraid to experiment with samples and see how different, yet relevant you can make the track. Force yourselves to improve and try new things. Curiosity can help you reach the pinnacles you plan to reach. Remember, Audiotool is a powerful tool... use it to your advantage.

tracks.name: Bad Habit
tracks.desc: When I heard this I felt almost invincible because of how epic it is! I'm a big fan of Arabian melodies and this track just blew me away. The vocals, the drop, the percussion . . . loved it all!

tracks.name: "Another life" Vocals by KrazieKammie (rec in AT)
tracks.desc: The artist in me was attracted by the cover of this song, but I discovered much more when I clicked the 'play' button. This was one of the first songs I've heard on AT and I was awestruck when I heard it. It's some of the best work from Audiotool community that I've heard yet. It's simply amazing.

tracks.name: Fresh Beatz
tracks.desc: As soon as I heard this I opened it up to study how Infy made this 'sonic heaven' of a track. Again, it's a combination of two styles/genres that are high on my list: Hip Hop and Dubstep. It's just amazing.

tracks.name: Bass Up
tracks.desc: This is definitely one of my all time AT favorites. Everyday, I strive to make EDM Trap tracks as epic as this one as well as some other tracks on Audiotool. The drop rendered me speechless. I don't think I could easily get sick of listening to this.

tracks.name: Solaris (feat. Luxior)
tracks.desc: What I love about this song is how chill yet powerful it sounds. The chord progression and melody were excellent. The work that was put into this track definitely shows.

tracks.name: Time Is Candy (with Ignapon3, Luxior, Almate, Teqtoniq and Kavil)
tracks.track_artist: SOLACE, Cuds, looks, Xavi, Lambda & Igna
tracks.desc: When I saw the title of this track, I knew it was going to be great; it wasn't what I expected when I first played it, but I fell in love with it. Needless to say, the track was very well produced, but my favorite part, the part that kills me is the drop tag; love it!

tracks.name: Sole Survivor
tracks.desc: The dark atmosphere of the track in the intro just keeps you suspended and waiting to see what happens. I loved the live-sounding drums, the chord progression, and how the track was arranged; it just moved me.

tracks.name: rabbit
tracks.desc: The serenity of this track captured my attention. I'm a big fan of piano tracks and calm/chill tracks so this one was the best of both worlds for me.

tracks.name: So Chill So Chopped
tracks.desc: Artist Recommendation. He's so talented and I think that his music should be reaching more audiences than it is at the moment. Where ever you are, keep doing your thing bro. You're the man!

tracks.name: Shield Of Fire
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. I just really like the drums and the chord progression. It's probably the best beat i've ever made on AT.

intro: In the person of Alain Groetzinger we have a real piece of music history in our midst. In the 70ies, as the drummer of the french Art-Rock band "Rockets" he sold millions of records. Today he inspires us with his banging Electro-Pop anthems full of soul and humanistic ideals. This week his autobiographical book "The Dream of Metal" is going to be released. One more reason for us to take a closer look at him. So should you too.
about:
Name: Alain Groetzinger
Place: I lived in Paris until 2013 and now I am in Britanny, in a small village.
Age: 59
Profession: Since 2007 I've been working for an animal welfare organization. I take care of everything related to advertising, newspapers, the Internet, all written and visual communication aspects.

interview.a: Hmm... I'm not sure I've got one. Overall, I like to switch between styles like Rock, House, Electro-Space and Downtempo. But I also like Athmosphere. It is wide but I love this diversification. But also, I almost always try to say something fundamentally human with voices which themselves are not at all human. It's sort of my gimmick.

I started playing the drums when I was 10 years old. The drum became my great friend throughout my adolescence. It was like a sort of permanent dream. Then I had the good luck to meet the band members of "Rockets" and I became their drummer. Yet another step in the dream. I was 19 years old. But in addition, the group bacame the number one foreign group in Italy. We sold more than a million and a half records. The dream became "The Dream of Metal." This name comes from the fact that we all had a metallic appearance, and all our instruments were metal. "The Dream of Metal" is also the name of the book that I have written, in which I tell the hole story. I hope they will do an english version too.

Then I went through a long period of immersion in Experimental music, noisy minimalistic, Electro. I called it "music to fall asleep". It was a real soul searching. I could spend hours listening to the sound of a drop of water or the noise of the wind. My delight was to play other sounds, one after another, without hearing the transition. Each sequence could take several minutes. But it was an amazing experience. If you try this exercise with the song of a bird and a hammer for example...

In 2011, after this great meditative period, I felt a great need to think of music from a different point of view: that of SHARING. Radically opposite to what I have lived for for 10 years. So in looking for a new opening, so a new adventure, I arrived on Audiotool. When I discovered all the possibilities of expression and sharing that this site provided, it was like a new artistic birth. For me, despite what I knew, it was just magical, revolutionary. It is still my view today.

My first message is to express my appreciation for all Audiotool musicians whatsoever whether they are famous or not. Among these artists ther are many references for me. Basically, what I often see is exactly what we do not hear: it is a kind of vibration that the musician allows to flow into his music without even realizing it. That's what I love. That's the magic of music. That's why I like certain artists, even if they do not have the professionality of some others. This, to me, is not of great importance.

My second message is to reaffirm the essential role of Audiotool for us all. But also the mind, the feeling, the openness and all the hard work of the team to ensure that Audiotool is such a possibility of expression. This, I think every time I open the application to find new music...

tracks.name: three minutes after the world died
tracks.desc: Tornsage is the flagship artist for me in music with the spirit of Downtempo or Trip Hop. I like it. I chose this track but I like many others of his, too. It is the simplicity, always with the vibration which is at the same time both tragic and exhilarating, wrapped in the spirit of Zen.

tracks.name: Under The Same Sun
tracks.desc: Olaf is the first artist whose atmospheric music touched me. I have always followed him and paid attention to his creations which are always of very good taste.

tracks.name: Personal Music - Esperience "Two"
tracks.desc: White Grizzly is a musician musically very close to the universe that I have explored for years with meditative music. So I have always looked out for new tracks of his, finding the atmospheres I've always loved.

tracks.name: Planes
tracks.desc: Julsy lies halfway between the very quiet music and the faster, more rhythmic tracks. But it's always in a Zen spirit which I like so much.

tracks.name: Stellar magnetic field
tracks.desc: It's Daftwill's frank to and fro between rhythmic and 'Electro Space' aspects with his characteristic synths and atmospheres which have made me love his music for such a long time.

tracks.name: Emancipation
tracks.desc: With Cihangir I glide with delight in the sensual and hot music into the depth of night. His music has always appeared exceptionally pure, both in terms of the spirit of its sounds, its arrangements ... well, for me it is a must.

tracks.name: Jonathan Garcia - baboom (Original mix)
tracks.desc: I have appreciated his music since he called himself kostetch. He, too, is for me a magical artist. I always liked his tracks.

tracks.name: Purple daze (2017 feat Intracktion)
tracks.desc: Sndbrgn is also one of the first artists whose music I liked and always followed. I am a great admirer of Kraftwerk and I found a not so distant vibration with Sndbrgn. I do not exactly know why, synths, mind, sound ... this good habit of going straight to 'the point'. Moreover, it is one of the first to have encouraged me to begin.

tracks.name: autoscontro
tracks.desc: Music composed by Cripta will serve me to build a bridge to the next track of this album. His style is also very wide-ranging, this is what I like. He always maintains an excellent musical taste, which in my opinion places him among the best sound designers of Audiotool. For me, this is a must-musician.

tracks.name: Rise
tracks.desc: So Jambosh is the one who I have always felt close to. I mean the style, sound etc. I've always loved the high quality of his tracks but also and above all the energy that is always there. This boy has always fired Audiotool with positive vibrations.

tracks.name: Pulverisateur Ep
tracks.desc: Artist Recommendation.

tracks.name: I Dance Naked
tracks.desc: Own favorite track. This is for the pleasure of hearing the voice of my friend Penny. A great time of collaborating, while we were 2,000 k apart

intro:
Oedipax and Jonjon, both AT legends of the first hour, demonstrate with their story how Audiotool can bring people together accross borders. Of course mostly this happens on a musical level. In this special case it led to more: Our congratulations go to Suzanne and Jonjon, who married in June 2014. We are proud to introduce to you the first intercontinental Audiotool couple.

Not forgetting that independently of one another they have played an outstanding role in the qualitative development of Audiotool music over the years.
about:
Oedipax
Name: Suzanne Greninger
Place: Seattle, Washington, USA
Age: 35
Profession: Administrative Manager at a nonprofit

Jonjon
Name: Jon Clark
Place: Seattle, U.S. (Originally from Coventry, England)
Age: 31
Profession: Self Employed Driver

interview.a:
Oedipax: I started learning classical piano at the age of 10 and have been playing ever since, occasionally branching off into some modern music.

Jonjon: I've never learned an instrument, but I tinkered with drums, lots of keyboards and a couple of synths as a kid. I grew up with lots of Reggae and Ska around through my father. Then one day, as a 10 year old, he bought me the Jungle-Mania cassette tapes and I was hooked! I would listen to the old Rave tapes at school, then I took up the turntables around 15, I taught myself how to mix and scratch records and spent much of my youth at record shops, club nights, raves and so on. I did a few DJ sets with my mate but nothing much. I haven't mixed a single record for over 5 years. Maybe I'll treat myself to some 1210's and start collecting old records again.

Oedipax: Right now, it's primarily dark and influenced by Industrial and technical/mechanical sounds and falling a lot within what people would call Drum and Bass or Jungle. I also dabble a bit with Jazz samples within a Jungle or Drum and Bass context.

Jonjon: Hard for me to say, but I believe in good drums that still have that older more acoustic sound and I love to pair a deep low end bass with the beat. I'll usually have some theme going and if I'm in the mood, I'll add a half decent melody.

Oedipax: I started regularly composing melodies and writing compositions on the piano at the age of 13, however, I found Audiotool in 2011 and started making electronic music at the ripe old age of 31! ;)

Jonjon: I was 18 years old and mostly used Reason

Oedipax: I found Audiotool through I believe what was Google's app store at the time and was introduced to electronic music production through this handy platform in early 2011.

Jonjon: In 2009 Luke recommended the original Hobnox site to me, so I uploaded some music and was actually really blown away by some of the musicians and video artists on there. Then I signed up for the first version of audiotool the night it went live. It was a few weeks before I published "Man in Grey" which was part of all those early tracks that I deleted.

Oedipax: Jonjon found me! ;) I saw that he faved one of my tracks on Audiotool and said something nice about it back in 2011. I clicked on his name, listened to a couple of his tracks and realized his great talent straight away. We started talking about music and both found common ground through our similar interests. Over the course of a year, we wrote about music, shared our thoughts about art, life, and many other topics, and learned from each other. Eventually, we decided we needed to meet in person. John came to the U.S. last year and stayed with me, and I found upon meeting him, it was like meeting someone I had already known forever. Before he had to go back to the UK, he proposed. I accepted wholeheartedly. Through the process of U.S. immigration and loads of research and paperwork on our part, he eventually was able to make it back here to the U.S. in May of this year. We married in June and we are living very happily together. :)

Jonjon: Warning: Have tissues at the ready. I was drawn to Suzanne from the moment I heard her early experimental tracks such as "Burn" and she was really into my afterglow remix. We began chatting on each others walls, then through email, video, pics, phone. Eventually we found we had written a huge romantic novel through e-mail! We share much in common and had experienced similar periods in life. We became best friends. Then we became romantic and I went over to Seattle a couple of times for the maximum period of time allowed. We got to know each other, we fell in love and had a lot of fun. I proposed to her at the beach one summer's evening, Suzanne accepted and then a week later, through many tears, I had to depart back to England and begin the whole immigration process which took an age, mountains and mountains of paperwork and much longing. Suzanne came to England for the first time to meet my family. When I got approved to return, I was the happiest man on earth. So then I went back for good, we married in June this year and we're enjoying life together very much.

Oedipax: Thank you all for all the music you make. Many of you are coming up with such innovative and incredible music. Keep doing what you do for the happiness you find in it. Many of you also know how much making music can be used as a creative and even personal outlet for emotions, dreams, and thoughts. Take it as an opportunity for exploration whether you want to express a deeper part of yourself or simply use it as an opportunity to learn more about certain aspects of music production. Whether you're just starting out or have been at it for years, remember that what you have within you is priceless regardless of popularity, fame, or recognition. Keep learning with fresh eyes and listening with fresh ears, but mostly, don't forget to have fun. ;)

tracks.name: It's Murder
tracks.desc: I love the big sound of 'It's Murder', the deep bass, and how that synth builds into a murderous climax. Bartone makes some killer drum and bass that can only leave you looking forward to hearing more

tracks.name: Last Man Standing
tracks.desc: I remember when I first heard 'Last Man Standing', I had to open it to see how he achieved it. CGMan has inspired many of my own creations and I love his powerful sound, monstrous bass, and incredible beats. He is, in my opinion, one of the most talented drum and bass producers on the site

tracks.name: Mesmerized Skin
tracks.desc: This was probably one of the first pieces on Audiotool that I listened to and I was immediately taken in by it. The title is fitting and the track has a beautiful hypnotic quality, taking you on a sonic journey that keeps evolving into otherworldly sound. EliaTrix is an artist of sound and mood.

tracks.name: epic acid
tracks.desc: Leave it to noraus to masterfully show us the potential of the 303 and many of the other tools of the AT app. Upon listening to 'epic acid', you can definitely hear not only the great sound engineer that noraus is, but the musicality of his work as well

tracks.name: REtaliation
tracks.desc: Tottenhauser's creations are made with a lot of enthusiasm and the quick energetic tempo, the machine-like sound effects, computer voice, and overall futuristic vibe simply transport me to another place and time, maybe even dimension altogether. I really get a kick out of music that accomplishes this

tracks.name: Amen Junglist
tracks.desc: I really like the deep bass in 'Amen Junglist' and the calming atmosphere of the rain sample and dreamlike synthesizer. I find that ascent is excellent at combining sounds and this track takes me to a chilled and relaxed state of mind

tracks.name: 8 Rules
tracks.desc: I've always enjoyed '8 Rules' and it keeps me moving to the rhythm. The placement of samples with the other musical elements is just awesome. The taking away and adding in of rhythmic and melodic components at just the right moments make for a track that you can appreciate when noticing the absences as much as the other elements that make it work

tracks.name: Hardjunk Reset
tracks.desc: I can not emphasize enough how incredible a producer 808Chunk is. Master of 303, Chunk has created a track that sounds like someone losing their mind at a rave. The absolute phenomenal energy and spectacular edits showcase his abilities.

tracks.name: Reaching
tracks.desc: Recommended Artist. While he's not on AT often, he always produces tracks of immense quality. I just love the chilled, beautiful vibe of this track. The vocal edits are expert. Just wonderful.

tracks.name: U
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. It mainly has to do with the cinematic sound of that recurring pad that I found in AT's sample library, and changing the pitch on it. I also really enjoyed playing with the Rasselbock as well. The track conjures up mystery, darkness, strangeness, otherworldliness, and even a bit of nostalgia for another time.

tracks.name: Too Far
tracks.desc: I have a lot of love for astrum's (now Zonra's) music. Another audiotooler who I watched grow and develop into the insanely talented producer he is now. He's made some great music over the years and it is good to see him continuing to develop his sound even further and push boundaries.

tracks.name: flying home
tracks.desc: Ralf is an experienced pro and distinguished sound designer. 'Flying home' has it all, soul.. infectious melody.. the right drums and it's mastered very well. I see this track as a work of art. I always return to it and for this reason it is my No.1 track made on audiotool, no doubt.

tracks.name: Expectation
tracks.desc: I've witnessed Tyburn (AKA Virtuous Duck) grow from day one and his rate of progress over the years is admirable. He is constantly impressing me. Tyburn produces an array of musical styles. I'm an avid Reggae fan, so Expectation gets my vote here.

tracks.name: M
tracks.desc: Of course, no list would be complete without a track from my beautiful wife! :) Suzanne's music blows me away, especially her Drum & Bass. Suzanne is also very conceptual and innovative with her compositions. Suzanne is an all round artist and because she is a genius pianist, this seems to add a unique and very different dimension to her electronic creations.

tracks.name: Reflected Reality
tracks.desc: A master of Techno, and of course master of the Pulve! Marco is one of my favorite producers. I remember his first track and he's been a true asset to audiotool and it's users over the years. He's inspired me a lot and i particularly enjoy his darker Dancefloor Techno tracks. NO FARCIO NO PARTY!!!

tracks.name: Survival
tracks.desc: A hugely talented young man with a very promising future as a producer. CGMan is one of the new breed taking Drum & Bass into the next chapter of it's evolution. His high octane music is exciting, dynamically perfect, yet savagely raw and robust.

tracks.name: pulsar
tracks.desc: Akimbo is my little Drum and Bass brother from Britain who I see a lot of potential in as an up and coming producer, he is very young but has already made huge leaps, especially with Drum and Bass.

tracks.name: bobby
tracks.desc: Another one I followed from the beginning. RNZR is an incredible producer, his tracks are filled with energy, infectious melodies and a lots of positivity. For me, 'Bobby' is a classic audiotool track and I still love listening to it very much. Legend!

tracks.name: Nimbus
tracks.desc: A recent track from my friend Mikel (formerly known as nowai). He has always had this talent for creating deep Techno and Jungle tracks, rich in elevating atmospheres. His sound has always reminded me of LTJ Bukem and been a rich source of inspiration for me over the years.

tracks.name: Aufbau Human
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. I could have done a list of at least 100 tracks and I have to give a special shout out to my friend EliaTrix, who is someone who makes very unique music and who has inspired me countless times. His music is artistically brilliant and every track by him resonates to my very core.

intro: We close 2014 with the remarkebly talanted Cally Kay from Ohio US. As one of the few female users she has found herself a place among the well known Audiotool artists. We would like to take this opportunity to encourage more female musicians to join our community in 2015. Come on girls!
about:
Caroline Kay Kennedy
Place: Ohio, United States of America
Age: 18
University: Business Marketing and Technology

interview.a: I've been singing ever since I could speak and at the age of 6 I took my first piano class for a year. I taught myself until I was 13 and then started back up taking classes from a professional pianist. My family is oriented around music. My father is teaching my younger brother to play the drums, my sister is teaching herself to play guitar by ear and she, my mother and I all sing. I am also currently taking piano lessons today and I recently had the opportunity to sing at a Veterans' Day Assembly with an audience of 1,300 people. On a normal basis, I lead the congregational music at my local church every Sunday and Wednesday Evening.

I would define my style as Trance, Techno, easy to listen to and danceable. Oh! And always optimistic. I have tried to make sad or evil sounding music and I just fail miserably. I also compare some of my tracks' styles to Owl City. They are always peppy, happy and encouraging. I make music to make people's day.
I did not start making music electronically until I found Audiotool. Before that I would compose music on my keyboard for leisure.

It was a cold and wet April Evening of my freshman year in high-school, so 3-4 years ago. I had recently downloaded Google Chrome because it was all the rage! I was playing around with the different music applications and came across Audiotool. It was everything I wanted and more. The community came together and helped me with almost anything I needed. People like Uprising, Tornsage, ZOD4X, Smily, and Inavon supported me and helped promote my music without me even having to ask them to. This has been the most brilliant experience and I never want it to end.

Thank you Audiotool, all the staff and producers and literally everyone who took part in helping make this site possible and running as smoothly as it does. I am so thankful for this, it is my number one hobby and favorite passtime. This site has changed my outlook on music and has helped me to discover what I would like to do in the future. As for the Community... Thank you to all my followers, fans, listeners and even to the haters! Your input helps me to be not only a better musician but even a better person in general. Thank you to the many more who like my me and my music and are supporting me. My advice to you is be creative, think outside the box, research, ask for help, experiment, be Optimistic, Love Chicken and <3 Audiotool.

tracks.name: Gone (EP is up for download!)
