I don't fall under any specific Genre, I experiment with many sounds, although I always try to create a sense of bliss and euphoria through my music, and I always try to include some type of Jazz.

I was born into a very musical family; My mother sang and played guitar, my grandfather also sang and played piano, drums and any kind of brass instrument you could think of, and I had a cousin named Ronnie Brown who used to play for a world-famous Avante Garde Jazz group named Sun Ra (lead Trumpeter). My grandfather was the one to teach me my first instrument (harmonica) and he really took it seriously whenever we'd sing songs in the car, so he gave me a better sense of Key and Tone. When I was about 11, I took my first band class and picked up the trumpet. I did learn much as far as trumpet goes, until I spent a week with my cousin Ronnie, who taught me, just whithin that week, everything I know about reading music. From there on, I played trumpet til I was about 13 and picked up the guitar. Shortly after, my mom bought me my first drumset. So essentially was learning guitar and drums at the same time, and got pretty decent at both. I still play guitar a lot, but I'm primarily producing on Audiotool now.
I started creating my own music, when I was about 13 years old. Writing lyrics and composing guitar chords to create my initial sound. over the years, my writing skills have developed tremendously.

I discovered Audiotool on a foggy morning when I was 16. me and a couple of stoners went back to a friend's house to get all lazy and surf the internet. I remember know how it got brought up but I do remember one of my girl friends saying "you know there's an app on chrome that you can make dubstep on right?". I imediately had a change in mood and asked over and over again to tell me the name of the app (Audiotool). Since then, I've been hooked. Making music and practicing every day on the app. I can almost remember my first "favorite" Who I believe was from OscarOllie (love you). Audiotool changed my entire life, and essentially gave me more purpose as a creator.

I only really have 2 things to say:

1. Thank you So much to the audiotool crew that helped make this DAW possible. I really can't tell you how much I appreciate having a free platform that still works as an authentic software to create professional production. Like I said earlier, it's changed my life in the most positive way, and now I feel like I'm at a place where I can really make a living off of it.

2. To the AT community: Follow your own path. Whether you want as many followers as possible, or just live to create a beautiful sound for your own purposes, FOLLOW YOUR OWN PATH. I'm personally trying to make it in the music industry and sometimes I have to ignore a lot of the AT users that I even look up to, just because their advice doesn't suit my path. So in the end, trust your gut, and make it whatever you want it to be. But also, PRACTICE. Practice practice practice and don't get lazy. Don't resort to only using loops that are off-key, really put time and effort into it, and understand that it is WORK and wont be easy at first; With that mentality, you wont be so overwhelmed. That's all I have to say.

tracks.name: Hybrid Heaven {bit-trøn rmx}
tracks.desc: This was Drax's entry to Lewi's remix contest. As non-complex as it is, the bass and chord structure is simply just in the right place. Something about it brings up this huge emotion that I can't quite pin point.

tracks.name: [Dem.Naps]
tracks.desc: Khari reminds me a lot of myself, what with how I use my samples. The big difference is that he's faster in development and is easily smoother than me. I remember when he was just known as 'sampler'.

tracks.name: Water Park Sunday
tracks.desc: Solace impressed me with this one. I've only known him to do DnB until I heard this track. Not only is the lead beautiful, but his drums are tight, the bass is unexpected and on point, and vibe is just chill.

tracks.name: im sorry
tracks.desc: Clu doesn't get enough credit for how versatile he is. Too many people call him 'future-bass' but there's a lot more to him. I've found myself very jealous of how he can accomplish what I've tried for years in just a few weeks.

tracks.name: japanese trapmaster
tracks.desc: I can't say much about kendrick, other than the fact that he makes bangers. Pretty underrated if you ask me. This song just spells B.A.N.G.E.R.

tracks.name: [Elegance.]
tracks.desc: Dzool is the guru of creating Lofi sounds through audiotool, which I know first hand is tricky. He really knows how to drown his sound in vinyl while keeping it very enjoyable.

tracks.name: morning sway
tracks.desc: Lewi is such a young talent. He doesn't get enough attention on his newer tracks. It's very soft and pretty, and the chords he uses always compliment the beautiful leads.

tracks.name: [Hybrid] Heaven []RMX[]
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. This was my unknown entry to Lewi's 'hybrid heaven' remix competition. Despite it being a remix, everything apart from the synth in the first 27 seconds was all me, NO SAMPLES.

intro:
dabrig was one of the early representatives of minimal Electro music on Audiotool. From his early raw approaches you could see him getting more complex and coherent in the structure and melody composition of his tracks, till his work reached its climax two years ago when he created timeless Audiotool masterpieces like "Distant Moons" and "Origin of Live".

Recently we have been hearing less of him but there is hope that this will change in foreseeable future.
about:
Pascal
Place: Hamburg, Germany
Age: 29
Profession: Who cares

interview.a: In my opinion I make hard, dark and atmospheric Techno music. Sometimes more or less Minimal-like. With a lot of Pulvs (from 5 to 7 is a lot for my computer) in it and focused on bass-synth, atmosphere and percussion. I love to spend hours on riding the knobs of the Pulv to find the one special sound I am searching for. Or I discover a totally different unique sound while I'm searching. But unfortunately this long process means my publish rate is almost zero today.

No musical education if you mean that. No instruments learned except the recorder in school. I don't think I can play it anymore. But I have always loved listening to music.

I always enjoyed hitting keys, drums and xylophones when I saw them until the time came I wanted to make music on my own.

I started making music very late, at the age of 22-23 I think. At that time I was listening intensively to albums like Stefan Bodzin's "Liebe ist ..." or Jean Michel Jarre's "Oxygene".

I loved these sounds and atmospheres so I wanted to create some of them myself. (a naive dream, as I have learned over the years) I got myself a free version of Music Maker and started but was getting bored after a year or so. All those sample and loop blocks, I hadn't a clue what to do with them. Only cutting, copying and pasting all the time ... so a musical out time followed. Then I found something on the internet... a mysterious machine called the Tone Matrix. I really started making music with Audiotool, learning by doing. I was scared by all those devices and knobs but year by year I got better to the point I'm at right now. Still learning more and more.

As mentioned above I found the Tone Matrix and some other cool mini tools on a website called 'laboratories'.

I played around for some days, which brought back my motivation to make music.

And then I wanted to find out more about the Tone Matrix which brought me to a site ehm ... I can't remember the name I'm getting old :)

But few a months later in march 2010 I found Audiotool. Back then when people like the crazydruminator, femme_robot or virtous duck were around ;) and Sandburgen had just a hand full of followers.

Thanks to all the people who worked on Audiotool for this unique DAW and to the community. Keep up the good work ... and all this without paying. Thanks Thanks Thanks ...

I could write a book of thanks and nice words but I'll keep it simple.

tracks.name: e.m.t.
tracks.desc: You ask why not the edit version... I can't explain. Perhaps nostalgia. But amazing track. I loved it back then and still so.

tracks.name: BABY LET ME
tracks.desc: Love this track. I listen to it often at work to get rid of bad mood.

tracks.name: moon_base
tracks.desc: One of the tracks that bind me to AT. Legendary track in my opinion but tastes differ.

tracks.name: C.N.C
tracks.desc: Amazing track. I really enjoy the 8bit-ish flavour of it.

tracks.name: Cembalo undead
tracks.desc: I think this the AT track I have listened to most and it made me a Sndbrgn fan.

tracks.name: Himmelblau (preview)
tracks.desc: Very creative and no less amazing track. I love the quiet sounds in this one and I don't agree with the 'pre'.

tracks.name: E-Voice
tracks.desc: Awesome track. I think it has the ultimate summer feeling. Listen to it a lot in the open air season.

tracks.name: closed
tracks.desc: A track I listen to a lot in private. A mind clearing track. When it starts the world turns a bit slower... cheesy.

tracks.name: wedoallthework
tracks.desc: A comparatively recent track on this list but unbelievably good. I especially love the middle part. Dreamy and danceably hard at the same time.

tracks.name: Hydrogen
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. Love the creativity of Bjoiful and the idea of his chemical elements series. Definitely underrated.

tracks.name: origin of life
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. Every time I listen to it I think it needs a better kick or some more work. But I won't change because for me the sound fits like little gears.

intro: E-Trim can't yet look back on a really long Audiotool career. But that's no reason for not taking a closer look at his nihilistically minimal approach. Whether he is letting off steam in physical Drum and Bass and Neurofunk tracks - in which milieu he obviously feels at home - or groping his way toward minimal Techno, he does both with the same intuition for dark but forceful Club Music.
about:
Bart Van Eynde
Place: Herentals, Belgium
Age: 36
Profession: High school Teacher (electrics, mechanics, CAD design, ...), no I'm not the enemy ;)

interview.a:
I mainly make bass driven dark drum n' bass and neurofunk, but occasionally I try some other things. Recently I'm also doing some dark minimal techno.

The best word to describe my style is "Dark". My goal on Audiotool is to one day make a perfect, pitch black track :)

When I was like ten years old I found an acoustic guitar on one of the closets at home. Intrigued by it I grabbed it and a little booklet with chord fingering fell to the floor. Since that day I tortured my fingertips every day learning the chord fingering and trying to make clean chord progressions. At the age of 12 I bought my first electric guitar and amp. A Fender Strat copy and an 80 watt Marshall amp. Years went by playing cover bands until my brother's punkrock band asked me to fill in for one of their guitar players. This interim job had gotten a little out of hand and I became a true band member. Some line-up changes (and new guitars) later we found a good singer which gave us the opportunity play all over Belgium, some gigs in the Netherlands and even France. Gaining local recognition, we even signed an underground Punk-Rock record label. We recorded 2 full Cd's and we even were the opening act for bands like Pennywise, Bad Religion, Rise Against ...

In 2013 I quit the band due to lack of time. I felt like I was holding down the band.

My father was a big rock fan so I grew up with great bands like Pink Floyd, Uriah Heep, Black Sabbath, Deep Purple etc ... I always listened to Grunge, Rock, Metal and Punkrock/hardcore music and later evolved to listening to Progressive rock and metal (the weird and complicated song structures became my new love). I always hated electronical music ... man ... was I wrong :D

At the age of 10 I started composing some little tracks on my guitar. As a 12-year-old I composed some more complex guitar tracks (still very basic compared to the sh#t I've composed later). Helped writing 3 full Cd's with my former band (last one I didn't record because I quit).

One day I had to replace a teacher that was on sick-leave. The students of that class didn't had a task so we just started a conversation about music genres and stuff and one of the students showed me Audiotool. At that time I was searching a tool to make drums and synths to make Progressive Rock/Metal myself and it seemed like a cool program. So I started experimenting with the synths and started listening to the published tracks. I learned allot from Infy's tutorials and it was so much fun I never even really came to the part of making Progressive Rock. Spent allot of time experimenting before I published my first track.

Good music always comes from the heart and there's no better way to do it with a (FREE) program that's build from the heart. I never used any other DAWs so I can't compare, but until now I always could make what I wanted to make. Sure there is some lag involved, but I blame my laptop for that ... not Audiotool :)

My advise to everyone is to make music for yourself and don't care to much about followers, favs and stuff. Sure it is nice and flattering but use this community to learn and share. Make friends, give real critique, collaborate, don't rush into making that next track. Make a track the best you can and then on a beautiful day u get featured ;)

Thanks to all Audiotoolers that have supported me during my journey. This feature has happened because of y'all.

Thanks allot Audiotool-crew for giving me an outlet to express my (mostly dark) feelings and spread it on this awesome community. And a big thank you for featuring me. I never thought it would happen to me.

tracks.name: Motorcycle
tracks.desc: This was the very first track on Audiotool that really blew me away. This very track is the reason why I started making dark bass driven Drum & Bass. CGMan created the ultimate bass in this one.

tracks.name: Dark Waters
tracks.desc: Darklite was most known for his Dubstep tracks but he's so versatile. The atmosphere and sound design of this masterpiece is so amazing. You can feel the darkness and pressure of the depths of the ocean.

tracks.name: Sword Of Angels
tracks.desc: Unkindness and Murder inspired me to try Techno myself. His tracks always have such driving vibe. The groove is just so great and I love how the bass seems to roll through the track.

tracks.name: Feat. twila.too - Dropping Now
tracks.desc: I could have chosen any track of the one and only Sndbrgn. I simply just love his synths and his layering. It's unmatched in Audiotool in my opinion. twila.too is Belgian like me.

tracks.name: Last Light Jaws Edit
tracks.desc: This list wouldn't be complete without a track from my good friend SharKyyo. I really love how the guitar and the amazing synths create this dark atmospheric, psychedelic feeling. I have always admired his 303 skills.

tracks.name: Summit
tracks.desc: I found this track by accident. It was one of the best miss-clicks I've ever made. Just listen to this beautiful track and let it take you away to divine places.

tracks.name: Bondage "Act 5"
tracks.desc: Another big composer on Audiotool that I admire is our big white teddy bear. No one can create dark ambient gems like he does. That intro drone just cuts through flesh and bones.

tracks.name: REsurrection
tracks.desc: I had to add a Tottmaster Drum & Bass track to my list. Not so long ago I found out that he is also Belgian. He's showing off his supremacy in drum manipulation. Let's make sure Audiotool doesn't forget us Belgians ;)

tracks.name: AeneA
tracks.desc: Maybe my favorite non-Drum & Bass track on Audiotool. This track left me sitting in front of my laptop with my mouth wide open, drooling all over my keyboard. It's gigantic. And you still owe me a new keyboard :)

tracks.name: 5erpent riv3r
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. I picked the duo C-men because of their name :D I like the way they experiment with sounds and synths. I think they have a lot of talent and they deserve more attention.

tracks.name: Mechanical eyes
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. I wanted to create an industrial feeling track. I like the energy and anger released in the drop and the fact that I was able to add a little melody.

intro:
Jacob Tyler is a real Audio pro. His tutorial templates are popular sources of good sound-design and effective production techniques.

The legendary contest series 'Audiotool Day' which he initiated starts its fourth season on November 10th and in all probability will far exceed previous year's participation. Just one of the reasons for looking by on Jake's Audiotool page.
about:
Jacob Tyler
Place: Hampshire, England
Age: 18
Profession: Southampton Solent University, studying to work as a sound engineer.

interview.a: I guess I don't have a specific style but I love messing around combining genres.
Violin for 10 years and played in numerous orchestras.
3 years ago. My first ever finished song was done with Audiotool.

I used to go round one of my friend's house all the time and he made music with Propellerhead's Reason. I didn't have the money to spend on Reason, so I went online looking for a free alternative. I was on the Google Chrome store and found Audiotool. The first time I used it I couldn't get any sound to come through unless I used the tonematrix. I got really annoyed and didn't revisit it for ages, and then I found some of Infyuthsion's tutorials on Youtube and I immediately became hooked.

Audiotool is an amazing site filled with amazing people. I'm at university studying to become a sound engineer, I work producing and recording music for other people as a career. I would never have even considered music as a career if it hadn't been for Audiotool. I guess what I'm trying to say is that Audiotool is a great place and that everyone should keep on doing what they're passionate about.

Also, I'm hosting the fourth annual "Audiotool Day" competition. Every year I upload a track with no content on it. You can then remix this track and you have until the 10th of December to make whatever you want. Last year I got over 5 hours worth of entries and it'd be awesome to see if we can get even more! I shall upload the track on the 10th of November, which allows everyone exactly 1 month.

tracks.name: Aaron + Nominal - Everything I want is far away
tracks.desc: This song is incredibly well produced! It's so chilled out and relaxed and the mix is so clean!

tracks.name: KTN-01
tracks.desc: This track has so much raw energy and all the samples fit together so well.

tracks.name: Watashi Zone
tracks.desc: This is such a gorgeous track. Even after years I always come back to this track. This was on the old Audiotool website and was featured on the homepage. Zone is one of the nicest guys on the site.

tracks.name: Cadmium Hotel
tracks.desc: This track's synth design is easily some of the best on the website. This collaboration by Flying Baby Seal and CGman never fails to impress me.

tracks.name: Flake
tracks.desc: This track's sound design is incredible. Every sound is perfectly timed and placed in the mix. A lot can be learnt from Olaf's sound design!

tracks.name: ./dream
tracks.desc: This song is so beautiful. It's so well composed and the vocals fit perfectly alongside everything else. After hearing it once I knew I had to remix it.

tracks.name: Sole Survivor
tracks.desc: This track was featured back on the old Audiotool website and I've never forgotten it. It is so 'big' and it's almost like an orchestra of synthesizers.

tracks.name: Dark Dub
tracks.desc: This is an extremely old school, dark Dub-Step track. Infyuthsion released it when everyone was trying to make over the top growls and massive drops, so this track was a breath of fresh air.

tracks.name: EVERYDAY ANYONE
tracks.desc: This track is incredible. The large reverb on every synth makes this track sound incredibly deep. The composition is fairly simple but it has been executed very professionally.

tracks.name: Ursa Minor
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. This track was the first track of mine to get featured and is responsible for why I continued producing to this day.

intro: Apollo Euphoria is not only a highly talented young musician. With his Label/Artist-Promotion project GenesisNetwork he has in a comparatively short time made a name for himself on Audiotool. We take this opportunity to congratulate him on his birthday and look forward to many more awesome tracks and projects.
about:
Ethan Lewis
Place: I was born in Indianapolis, Indiana; raised in San Diego, California; and am currently finishing up school in Orlando, Florida
Age: 16
School: I'm a sophomore in High School

interview.a: I really enjoy Electro/Complextro tracks in or outside of Audiotool—along with some occasional progressive house, melodic Dubstep, etc. However, my personal production style seems to be more geared toward Chill Progressive/Trance. I'd like to, however, begin making uplifting progressive house/trance and electro in the future, as that is my ultimate goal with production.

I think I have a quite extensive musical background. Growing up, I remember listening to country music. Perhaps it was my surroundings that accounted for that. Regardless, it didn't stick around for long.

After my parents separated, my mom moved to literally the other side of the country in California. When I moved there with her, I didn't have much technology, so I turned to the radio for music. There happened to be one station that played music from the 70s and 80s that I really liked (also, mostly because I refused to conform to the contemporary standards of Top 40s music). A few of my particular favourites that I could actually recall included Earth Wind & Fire, Billy Paul, Rick James, you get the gist.

Not long after, and completely contrasting the contemporary, my mom gave me and my sister this CD that had music on it from as early as the 1930s. Big Band, it was called. It was the music my grandfather used to listen to, and it really spiked my attention... it was just good-natured, great sounding, upbeat music that I found myself clinging to for awhile.

Afterwards, Pandora relatedly got me into more jazz-like music. Michael Bluble's modern renditions of Frank Sinatra and whatnot.

Following that—and actually one of the most pivotal points in my life, I got my hands on the pirated version of Minecraft. The game I absolutely loathed one day, and came to become an extreme enthusiast of the next. I can remember loading up a new world on my school laptop; when the music started playing, I already knew what the game had to offer. It took me a good while to stop playing the game altogether, but during my addiction to it, I learned as much as I possibly could about it; and getting to know C418—the musician behind all of the music in the game, documentary, etc.—and his background in music from the 2012 Mincecon Stream really enlightened me to become what I am today. As I look back on it, truly, if I hadn't gotten into Minecraft, I likely wouldn't be into producing either.

From then, and after finding the SoundCloud platform, I started listening to some really generic dubstep just by searching the tag through the site. (Back then, they didn't even really have tags) Nonetheless, I found myself starting to become more engaged with music itself. With prior experience with various genres, SoundCloud became an outlet for me to find artists such as AdhesiveWombat, Sparx, and Sanixels, who—still to this day in my opinion, are some of the best electronic producers out there.

Lastly, as you know, being into Minecraft I was pretty involved in the YouTube community regarding that select part of the platform for gamers. I often watched quite a few of CaptainSparklez's videos and he always has an outro with a really interesting track. So, one day, I deceided to find out who was the artist of the track. As it turns out, it was Stephen Walking's "Top Of The World" signed to a label called Monstercat that I began listening to promeniently for a good year or two. Only now am I trying to diverge from its confinement and listen to other artists, try finding them on my own. It's difficult, but worthwhile in the end.

I've been producing music since—really when I purchased a horrid toy keyboard after receiving a gift card for my birthday. I think I was about 7 or 8, but I remember messing around with different songs, chords... just very basic... crap in general. Soonafter, however, being extremely curious and enticed by what I was hearing, and wanting to improve it, I began trying to make my own music. Looking up free ways to do so online, I found Soundation and started dabbling with that. I probably won't go into too much detail about that, but I mean, if you really want to damage your brain, I guess you could check out the site and search "MineatronOrigin" (Which was my Minecraft name).

My mom started picking up on my ear for music and tried to harness it, putting me in piano lessons early on. Unfortunately, I didn't take most of the opportunities seriously and ended up loosing them. As life goes I suppose. However, it wasn't until last year that I started being pretty much 100% dedicated to production, on Audiotool especially.

Right after getting fed up with Soundation, I searched a little harder and found Audiotool... That's really all there is to it. Nothing too extravagant. Of course, when I first discovered Audiotool though, I didn't even know there was a community to interact with, so most of my first tracks went unnoticed as I didn't bother to tag anything.

GenesisNetwork is my Audiotool-based music network that is working everyday to find and showcase other artist's talent on the platform and bring out the best in each artist providing feedback and in-depth analysis of their tracks.

Of course, the network is still in it's early stages and we're still establishing the groundwork. However, once we're fully up and running, I would have also acquired a team to maintain it (I'm currently the only one working on it at the moment).

How it works is artists submit tracks to us through email or rabbit and we determine whether or not they made it in. If they did, they will either be in the current or previous wave. If they didn't, we will do our best to provide constructive feedback on the track they submitted, and they can try as many more times as they'd like.

Each wave in Genesis consists of about ten artists and each artist must release at least one track of their own (not including collaborations) before the wave is complete. Once a wave is finished, previous artists that joined are not allowed to release through Genesis again. There will be a reunion for those who released previously. At the end of a wave, there is a remix competition of every track released through Genesis to determine future artists and to engage the community.

Keep working hard and help each other out. I believe there are amazing people out there waiting to be heard, they honestly cannot do it without your help. Not only that, but I believe we can make this community more connected and a powerful resource for producers across the globe. It already is, in some ways, but there's always room for improvement. You don't have to do something huge to help out either, it could be as simple as giving helpful feedback on a track, regardless of the quality mainly because you don't know really who's behind it. They may just be wait for the opportunity to become something better.

I remember being that person.

tracks.name: Running from the Wind
tracks.desc: Some artists on Audiotool don't get enough attention, despite how many followers they have. Xtraction shouldn't be one of those artists. His music is amazingly unique and inspiring.

tracks.name: Annihilate Ft.Neocterix [Melodic Dubstep]
tracks.desc: When he published 'Complextro [Urban-Contact Style]' almost a year ago, I already heard his unharnessed potential. He pretty much, single-handedly controls the charts.

tracks.name: Lost In Time
tracks.desc: I believe Dollavix is misunderstood on the part of the Audiotool community. He's a really talented artist and although he uses other explicit elements from other artists' tracks, he still manages to put his own little twist on his work.
It was only recently that I heard TheAnonymousPineapple's works. We have quite a bit in common in terms of knowledge of music theory.

tracks.name: Silence In Tokyo
tracks.desc: Joining in on a few rabbits in the past I was able to find this prodigy who has a ton of amazing works coming in the future. He deserves to be on this list more than I can say.

tracks.name: To Feel Alright (Feat.ENY)
tracks.desc: This is simply a breathtaking track. I'm no good with working with vocals, and I can sense the hard work put into this track. I can definitely see orcean going far with music.

tracks.name: is this
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. Mirai is an incredible, young, aspiring producer that has so much to offer. This track in particular helped me keep going in times when I didn't think I'd amount to anything, in life and in music.

intro: Another talented youngster in the Audiotool Artist Crew: Akimbo delivers polished Club Music with a focus on driving Drum &Bass. And that with a stylistic confidence which makes it hard to believe he is at the beginning of the road. Though he describes himself as an amateur on his AT account, many of his tracks wouldn't be out of place on an official publication. Be sure to check his music out, his future seems promising.
about:
Jack Hughes
Place: Bristol, UK
Age: 17
School: Still at school

interview.a: Drum and Bass/UK Bass

I don't really have a musical background to be honest. I played a bit of guitar and piano, and listened to a lot of Classic Rock as I was using my dad's iTunes account most of the time: Led Zepp, Van Halen, The Happy Mondays, those kind.

Before Audiotool. I had some guitar and piano lessons, but I never really mastered either instrument, although my dad did teach me enough guitar to jam with him, although it was only the 12 bar and some basic chords. Most musical theory that I've learned has actually come from opening up other users tracks and seeing how they've done certain things, and then trying to copy them.

I discovered Audiotool when I was about 12, and used to play around with it regularly. But I never really published anything until I was 13, about 4 years ago now. Some of the stuff actually received a positive reception, and from then I was hooked! I've loved seeing artists on this site flourish into some of the most followed users, and the site itself evolve while I watched.

The one thing that I'd like to see more of on Audiotool is more constructive criticism of tracks. At the moment it seems like some tracks only get praise where as a little constructive criticism could make them a lot better. I wouldn't mind someone telling me how to improve my music, and I'm fairly sure that other people wouldn't mind either. So if you can see something that might need improving, just say.

tracks.name: kitsch
tracks.desc: RNZR always delivers, but this track just edges out all of his other stuff. Those chords and the percs are absolutely on point.

tracks.name: Liquid Drum and Bass 3
tracks.desc: This track changed the way that I thought about making D&B. It was so insanely smooth and almost nostalgic while still keeping the energy that I loved. This was my main inspiration making Technicolour.

tracks.name: Blue Days feat. Emma
tracks.desc: Quality Deep House from cihangir. Love the vocals as well.

tracks.name: shudder [IN NAME ONLY EP Nov 15, for real this time]
tracks.desc: NOMINAL has always had some really interesting tracks, but I love the nostalgic feel that this track has, with the main chords sounding distant but always present. Insane stuff.

tracks.name: Find a place right here
tracks.desc: I love the 80's vibe of this track. It's a bona fide AT classic.

tracks.name: Love In An Empty Shell (Mikel Boyles Mix)
tracks.desc: Love this remix. Offbeat Ninja is one of my favourite Hip Hop/Bump artists on Audiotool. But this remix is so much better than the original, which was a really good track in its own right.

tracks.name: young
tracks.desc: Proper lovely stuff. Amoeba combines his natural ear for melody with a slightly harder edge than a usual amoeba track, and it pays off really really well.

tracks.name: Big Narstie - Gasleak (FSC remix)
tracks.desc: Don't judge. This goes in hard.

tracks.name: find me
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. His mix down and drums could use some work, but he has a fantastic ear for melody, and surprisingly good tracks for a guy with 20 followers. I love the melody/bass combo.

tracks.name: Jonjon - Breach of Trust (Akimbo and Jambams future Jungle Remix)
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. I love the way that this one turned out. Jambam's chords in the second half top everything off, so he gets props for this as well.

intro: When in 2012 a young man called "Erdbeerquark" (strawberry curd) entered the Audiotool stage a murmur went through the virtual crowd. His very first piece went straight into the track feature of the old website. His chilled Downbeat tunes are always spot on. His atmosphere constructions enriched with his surrealistic guitar sounds draw the listener under their spell leaving behind a blissful sense of contentment.
about:
Aaron Schäfer
Place: Berlin, Germany
Age: 19
Profession: I am Studying Music-Technology/Sound Engineering in Berlin.

interview.a:
Many people call my music "Ambient" and I agree to some points. Maybe because of my guitar atmospheres. But all in all it's different to real Ambient.

It's hard to define my style, I would call it Chill, Deep, Experimental.

This year I have started to record my electric-guitar and I love playing around with it.

A huge inspiration to me was Eivind Aarset (a Scandinavian guitar player).

He always makes those big room sized guitars with a lot of reverb, which brings an awesome atmosphere.

I also love working with vocals. I am not very good with the AT synths, mostly I use them in a really simple way for some sub bass, for example.

Inspiration: Olaf. He makes awesome beats. And I love his guitars. Even he hasn't published any tracks in the last 2 Years on Audiotool. He is still one of my favorite Audiotool Artists.

I started making music when I was about 12. I played the guitar and sang in a school rockband, Just for fun. I was into Nu-Metal, Rock, and Brit Pop at that age but my music tastechanged when I found out about Audiotool in 2012. My Father told me about Audiotool. And I just went ahead. I didn't have any experience with DAWs.

But here Audiotool helped me to get into it really fast. And I learned so much from others in the Community. Just because of Audiotool, I started listening to electronic music. But I always wanted to bring something natural into my music. So I started recording my own guitar lines. I hope to get better soon.

I think Audiotool is the perfect place to have fun and let your creativity run wild, and I've found there are so many things you can do with electronic music that you can't with the classical approach to writing music for instruments and stuff. When you're writing electronic music I would advise you to listen to lots of different electronic artists and also listen to classical music. Listening to electronic music really helps me find inspiration for different sounds I could make, different textures and stuff, and the classical music helps me with the actual compositional technique; I try to get into the composers head and wonder what they were thinking, why they are doing what they are doing with the music. Just listen and learn from what is out there, keep doing it until you get better and above all have fun. :)

tracks.name: leave.
tracks.desc: An artist who is really underrated in my opinion. I found his music a few weeks ago, and fell in love with it.

tracks.name: Hue
tracks.desc: Also one of the most underrated artists on Audiotool. Love his beats. Mostly when they are percussive, with those nice wood sounds like in 'Hue'.

tracks.name: Passive
tracks.desc: This track is one of my all time favorites. Feels like it came out yesterday but it's already two years old. This track shows that even a simple track can have a lot of power.

tracks.name: Sunloop
tracks.desc: I have listened to this track 500 times AT LEAST! On long Bus trips it helps me to sleep. It always warms my heart with the beautiful guitar.

tracks.name: BGN {OVR VIP)
tracks.desc: I love the beat, the bass, the stuttered synth and the dry snare. Everything fits perfectly together.

tracks.name: Nor
tracks.desc: This is a shout out to all of the other members of our collab. They motivated me most in the last months. This one, definitely is my favorite Untrust track.

tracks.name: Let me take you away
tracks.desc: He is my favorite Ambient/Chill artist on Audiotool. The crackle in combination with the percs and the whisper... I love it <3

tracks.name: there's always tomorrow
tracks.desc: A really long and great intro. The drop with the Kalimba is even better.

tracks.name: My room is a mess,
tracks.desc: The combination of the beat, the chilled vibes with the acoustic bass. I LOVE IT! So underrated!

tracks.name: Untitled II
tracks.desc: This is Ambient to my mind. I always listen to this when the first snow falls. Sentimental moment....

tracks.name: Electronica [Double run]
tracks.desc: I listened to this last week. So underrated!

tracks.name: Strahlen [Chaos]
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. It's a perfect example of what I am doing now. I am using my guitar in different ways, layer them up to 5 guitars and do special rhythms (11/4 in this case). This is always an inspiration for my upcoming tracks.

intro: The impressive thing about Floydjasper is not only his unfailingly clean production and the outstanding atmospherics of his tracks - every second of which bears witness to his skills. But also his approach to other artists works and his way of commenting on them reveal his knowledge and love of music. An artist we can just warmly recommend.
about:
Jack Mills
Place: Born and raised in Rhode Island, USA
Age: 23
Profession: Design-engineer at a bio-medical company, no degree.

interview.a:
haha:) In the words of the ever-poetic dronealpha, "genres R 4 critics."

But to answer the question in a less annoying way, I like warm analog tones, heavy grooves, polyrhythms and minor 7th and 9th chords:)

Classical guitar from age 5, played recorder, violin in school. I've played bass, guitar and drums in bands ranging stylistically from metal to hip hop to folk and everything in between. It's only been in the past couple years or so that I really seriously have gotten into electronic stuff. I wish I could play trumpet.
I started playing when I was 5, but didn't actually start writing my own music until I was about 12.
My wall says "ABOUT 4 YEARS AGO". The cracked DAW I was using gave up the ghost, so I started googling 'browser based sequencer'.
Stop screwing around on facebook and go make some music!

tracks.name: ultraviolet
tracks.desc: Opaqity is one of my favorite AT artists. I don't understand how he makes his tracks so smooth and jazzy while claiming to know nothing of jazz composition. His attention to detail and worshipful approach to melody and harmony never fails to impress.

tracks.name: conga_jams_01
tracks.desc: Another super raw track from another of AT's finest outsiders. The use of noise and ambiance in this track is so good it hurts. Lo-fi at its lo-finest. VLTRA's got that 'so sloppy it's tight' shit down to a science.

tracks.name: tijuana
tracks.desc: Torn was one of the first 'big names' in the AT community to give me the time of day. He is also a major supporter of emerging artists. The mood of this track is amazing, super grimy. I can feel the air rippling like water in the heat of the sun when that trumpet sample hits.

tracks.name: Night Train
tracks.desc: One of my earliest favs as an AT user. The way that delirious bass-line pulls the groove along is incredible. The delicate use of space in this track is perfect.

tracks.name: Finding the words to say to you
tracks.desc: This track completely blows my mind. So loose, yet so much groove, and the harmonic structure is utterly merciless.

tracks.name: premaster001
tracks.desc: I don't 100% know who this is. Appears to be an alternate account. It really takes a lot of confidence and vision to take a track as simple as this from start to finish. Minimal enough to evoke a sense of starkness, but enough textural and groove development to keep heads nodding.

tracks.name: chill tribe vibes interlude 10m Boiler Mix
tracks.desc: Finally the great Lewi-Astro. This track is just way too cool for school. Perfect mixing proportions, so much detail. Definitely one of the chillest tracks on AT. I'd also like to thank Lewi for the amazing sample collection he has made available to the AT community.

tracks.name: Hydrogen
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. I don't know if this dude's still making tracks, but he/she/it has a bunch already that are excellent. I especially love the idea of tracks inspired by the periodic table. Support the AT underground!

tracks.name: semiquaver
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. I've been doing a lot of 4 on the floor lately, felt like switching things up. I'm really happy with the marriage of the kick drum and bass in this track.

intro: The list of Psy Trance musicians on Audiotool isn't the longest. Brainwalker definitely belongs to its pioneers and flagships. In every single note of his output you can feel the experience he has collected over the years he has been into this genre and music in general. A must at every Goa event!
about:
Stefan Holzner
Place: Augsburg near Munich (Germany)
Age: 40
Profession: This year I completed a training as a nurse for handicapped people. Before that I was a businessman for 20 years, but it doesn't suit me anymore ;-)

interview.a: Oh, that's not easy – my tracks are inspired by many different styles, from Psy-Trance, Dub, Retro, 8-bit to Rock and Pop - it depends on my mood and different influences in my life… The main genre which I will produce more of in future consists of all kinds of Psy-Trance… The other genres inspire me, but Psy-Trance is what I really love :)

I never learned any kind of instrument… About twenty years ago I bought a Djembe and played with friends in nature and at Cosmic-Music Festivals. I think that was my first serious contact with a musical instrument… Later a friend loaned me an electri -guitar for some months and I explored the great sounds and rhythms, but I never had guitar-lessons.

I started to make music when I was roundabout 13. I played around with an Music-Tracker called "Pro-Tracker" on the Amiga 500. I was totally fascinated by creating my own music. I made lots of short tracks, recorded them on cassettes and shared them with friends. In the middle of the 90's I started to buy some cheaper hardware synthesizers and keyboards to produce my own Techno and Trance-stuff.

Two and a half years ago, my girlfriend asked me if I could look for good and cheep music programms for her, because she wanted to check out making music… I searched for a few hours in the web – and tadaaa: I found Audiotool!

I was really surprised by it's possibilities and since that time I have been inspired to make music with Audiotool. The crazy thing about this is, that I never wanted to produce with software, because I tested one of the usual suspects and it worked against my creativity, but Audiotool convinced me with its easy and clear handling.

For me Audiotool is seriously one of the best tools for producing music. Because it supports my way to be creative. Other kinds of music software are overloaded with possibilities and less intuitive things. I think my creativity gets pushed, if my options are limited – and I definitely have more fun this way! Other DAWs may have 10.000 options and you can download tons of plug-Ins – but that way I never came to an end ;-)

On the other hand I really like the community! I found a few friends there and sometimes it's surprising how much some people improve in a few months! It helps me to go on with my music and some comments helped me to improve myself – thanks a lot!

Sometimes I wish other producers would respect Audiotool! I know so many really good artists on this site – and often the only thing they can say: what – no plugins? – than I won't try it – but it's your fault guys ;-)

tracks.name: missed generation
tracks.desc: This track was my first earworm on Audiotool for a long time... It is magnetic and one of the best tracks on Audiotool in my opinion...

tracks.name: Transoceanic
tracks.desc: This tune transports a lot of energy and mood. I think this track of The Seahorse really shows his potential and his wide musical background...

tracks.name: Royal Flush
tracks.desc: This track is so powerful and magnetic that I had to hear it lots of times. Even though it's not my favorite music genre...

tracks.name: OBJeCT
tracks.desc: This track got a beautiful atmosphere and it's constructed quite interestingly. Furthermore it uses effects in a perfect and effective way, especially the reverb...

tracks.name: The Truth
tracks.desc: Oh my good – what a track! It shows perfectly that you can create real professional sounding Psy-Trance on Audiotool! What a pity that Sonic Particle isn't publishing at the moment.

tracks.name: Ravage
tracks.desc: Another Psy-track that inspired me a lot! I think this was the first real Psy-track I explored on Audiotool. It shows how good some fx-samples work in Trance-Tracks...

tracks.name: Banisham
tracks.desc: Cripta was the first person on Audiotool I had contact with. He makes great electronic tracks, but when he published this Psy-track, I was really fascinated...

tracks.name: flatliners
tracks.desc: A powerful track full of pressure! For most percussions and drums he used synthesizer and not samples – a quite interesting sound...

tracks.name: Meerbusch
tracks.desc: This is my latest earworm on Audiotool! It sounds really magnetic and shows the art how to make minimal music that never sounds boring, even if the same melody runs through the whole song...

tracks.name: Geometric Abstraction
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. He is in my opinion the most underrated musician on Audiotool. This track shows perfectly his Psy-Trance skills enhanced with his own creative, futuristic influences.

tracks.name: evolutionary humanism
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. This is not the best track of mine, but its message seems very important to me! Currently very bad and inhuman things are going on in Germany and Europe... we are one!!!

intro: Hardly another name has appeared so often on our wall in the last few months: Everyone wants Zerod! Reason enough for us to take a closer look at this young man: flawless EDM music with a tendency towards melodic Dubstep and an occasional touch of frenzy. That's what Zerod is about.
about:
Alejandro Ybarra
Place: Mission, Texas (USA)
Age: I am currently 15 years old
School: I currently attend High School

interview.a: It's hard to define my style. I like to mix it up sometimes, but most of my tracks are melodic Dubstep. It's what people know me the most for.
I started to listen to Electronic music since I was a young kido. I was around 7 to 9 when I fully got into it and really enjoyed listening to it. Most of it was Electro, Trance, Dubstep, etc.

I started to OFFICIALLY make music somewhere around September of 2014. I wasn't that good, but to see how much I've progressed in the span of a year (and a few months) I am highly impressed with how much I've learned and have had experienced. I'm proud to have ran into Audiotool.

I really wanted to start making music when I was 12. I started messing with DAWs that to this day I cringe hard to. I really couldn't do it so I somewhat gave up. I kept trying to find a DAW that I could really use to actually make music on. FL, Ableton, Cubase, and all those other fancy DAWs cost money. I searched up free DAW and found Audiotool. I decided to take a shot at it. I wasn't disappointed with that I was capable of doing on there.

Audiotool is such an amazing site. The community is really not just a bunch of artists but more of a family to me. I honestly wouldn't be where I am on Audiotool if it wasn't for the community who helped me along the way. They taught me a lot. I really enjoy what the DAW has. The Pulverisateur (I can't spell), the Heisenberg, the giant sample library, the CPU usage that ends up destroying my computer and makes me buy a new one. This DAW is very unique. And I honestly can't leave it for another DAW. Sure I may get another one but eventually Audiotool will come back. Audiotool ALWAYS comes back. :)

tracks.name: Neo Tokyo
tracks.desc: Every song I hear from xav is mind blowing and inspiring. This was one of his songs that got to me the most. His chord progressions are always so nice and very impressive.

tracks.name: silver palm
tracks.desc: One word I can use to describe opaqity's tracks: Percs. This would have to be my favorite from him. The chords, the melody that plays in the track overtime, and even the white noise. I really loved this track when I first heard it <3.

tracks.name: Ubongo
tracks.desc: Anything from CGman just proves what Audiotool is capable of doing. Every song he makes has some pretty crazy atmospheres and Neuro basses. Every time I hear one of them, I am amazed.

tracks.name: Contraflux
tracks.desc: This was one of the first songs I heard on Audiotool. It's what got me into Electro House stuff. Lux's Electro House was something I truly miss.

tracks.name: Purple
tracks.desc: I sometimes listen to chill sort of stuff. I really enjoyed this when I first heard it. The pads. The bass. Teqtoniq's stuff amazes me every time. Hope to see him get into medical school soon! :D

tracks.name: bloom
tracks.desc: I've known Neo for quite a bit of time, and I've always enjoyed hearing his stuff. I've never been able to picture him making chill music for once. The drums, the melody, and let's not forget the arp <3

tracks.name: these aren't tears of joy
tracks.desc: Over time the track really started to get to me. Dove's LFO pads in this were pretty satisfying to me. And of course... the bubbles :)

tracks.name: LIMITLESS
tracks.desc: Of all songs I've heard from this guy I instantly fell in love with this one. The supersaws, the lead, the little arp that plays at some parts of the track. But go a bit easy on the compression next time BSRK!

tracks.name: Waves
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. I seriously have to thank zelvo for showing me this guy. Holy crap this guy is good. Those vocal chops, the chords, the sound design in this song was pretty good.

intro:
You only have to look at the number of tracks that he has produced – and the following he has amassed – to see that Sandburgen is one of the cornerstones of the Audiotool.

Rooted in Minimal Techno and incorporating elements from Trance and Electro, he has crafted his own individual style that makes his tracks both distinctive and instantly recognisable.

He encourages other users by consistently producing high quality tracks that demonstrate the full potential of Audiotool and set the standard for others to aspire to.

[Mark 'Shakey' Valleley]
about:
Ralf Noetzel
Place: Düsseldorf (Germany)
Age: 43
Profession: Executive chef in an event catering service

interview.a:
My style has its roots in the Minimal and Detroit Techno of the 1990s. Influences from deep Retro Electro, mournful Indie music and of course everything that I listen to on Audiotool have created the style you know me for.

Artists like Dominik Eulberg, Stimming and Extrawelt and many more have been producing more melancholic dance music for some time now. So Sandy style is not really my invention.

The outstanding soundtrack of the movie 'Drive' motivated me some years ago to dip into the darker 80s influenced Electro Pop. This was the foundation for Cripta's and my collaboration project 'Sandcripten' which is still one of my favorites.

Kids Drum Set - Skip - Recorder - Skip - Classical guitar - Skip - Bassguitar in the band of the Music School of my hometown - Skip - Drums in some Post Punk and New Wave Bands - Skip - And then finally the 90ies:

After a few years as a guest at diverse raves and club parties it was just a matter of time till I wanted to take an active part in the Techno thing. So I started buying records - mainly crappy mainstream Trance anthems. The first DJ gig followed soon. And that although I didn't actually have the faintest idea what it was all about. That was to change when after an appearance at the AJZ Bielefeld I was introduced to a guy who had already made himself a name in the scene of the town. After the party was long since over André Michelle and I stood in the deserted club and took turns spinning records.

Musically I would say that I gained more from this encounter cause from this day on it was clear that Detroit Techno and Acid was the thing not the Rave stuff that I had up my sleeve.

After this our paths were inseparable. We invested in a small home studio which was not to stand still for a long time. Later my brother, who you may know as Olondro entered the scene. And so we published a considerable number of records under our - unfortunately rather short-lived - labels Matrix, TINT and Convenience. It seems marketing was not our main talent.

At the beginning of the millenium, after our crew had dispersed across the country I started working with DAWs like Reason and Cubase and some of my tracks were published under a Cologne based label called Pinku. But that all petered out. I stopped spinning records and I turned to producing sounds for browser games and websites. That earned me a bit of cash but killed the last motivation to produce my own tracks.

But then Something happened which was to put everything in place again:

André who had meanwhile landed up in Berlin decided to come to Cologne, bringing with him a project that sounded like the fulfillment of his (and all our) dreams.

To be honest, as long as Audiotool belonged to Hobnox I didn't play so much with it. The impossibility to save and publish and the restricted choice of instruments didn't immediately made to take it seriously. Not until the range of instruments grew and André converted me by showing me rnzr's "teeth" and his "8 Bit but a Bite" did I start to get hooked. Five years and about 250 tracks later I can say that I as an old hand was able to learn a lot.

I want to thank the handful of guys whose enthusiasm has made Audiotool possible. It plays an irreplaceable role in the lives of many of us.

It has turned out to be more than just a DAW. It has become a platform for creative thinking in general and has inspired many users to start up further projects and collaborations. And that's what the community has achieved. Stay classy and carry on being awesome!

And of course thanks for the vote!

tracks.name: Lonesome
tracks.desc: Every new attempt (it felt like 1000) to compile this album had one thing in common: This track. With a single Bassline 3 and FX automations which I still can't comprehend, Someone created something that most of us couldn't even have managed with a full orchestration.

tracks.name: Lost
tracks.desc: Well deserved winner of the Moogfest Contest. How the hell did he get all this out of only two Pulverisateurs? Kepz also would have been on any list of mine. His stuff is not only very well produced, it also meets my taste in 99.9% of cases.

tracks.name: move
tracks.desc: There is no 100% pure Audiotool list without Mr Standfast. In very few of his tracks are there any signs of samples. His synth melodies are unmatched not to mention what he does with the drums. That's Mr Standfast.

tracks.name: Verlangen
tracks.desc: One of my first real favorites on Audiotool (and what luck without samples). A perfect symbiosis of a cool Minimal beat and atmospheric synth lines. And that bass! Here I first understood the Tonematrix as a respectable production tool not just a toy.

tracks.name: Nowhere But Everywhere
tracks.desc: Freshest addition to the list: heartwarming Trance synth melodies and... isn't that a choir? In the middle of the track a little organ solo conjures up a smile and then sends us back to the dreamy melancholia of this piece of art.

tracks.name: Strange Small Blue Box Dub
tracks.desc: We don't have to say anything about Noraus' qualities as a sound designer. The glitchy hi hats, the percussions and the fat kick drum, all synthesized with the Heisenberg is just indescribable. And on top of it all it is simply an awesome Dub track.

tracks.name: dark melody (fbs mix)
tracks.desc: What FBS has done with shoes' small but no less beautiful Tonematrix melody is remarkable. When the Rasslebock adds the bass, the dark sun rises.

tracks.name: ethereal
tracks.desc: Great Neo-Trance piece without a single sample. Christian Chrom has some of this kind on his tracklist.

tracks.name: Photomultiplier
tracks.desc: Another sound master: Tyburn. Here too no trace of drum computer. Instead 100% Pulverisateur. A stroke of genius in my eyes. A little more Tyburn wouldn't hurt in future.

tracks.name: one pulver only
tracks.desc: One Pulve, one reverb and a compressor. Not the setup you would expect behind this fresh and funky milestone. This is really worth opening, there is something to learn here.

tracks.name: Deep Inside
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. His track 'Deep Inside' delivered the initial spark for the 100% Audiotool idea. This guy has worked his way through the material in the past two years like hardly another. A monster of a downtempo track.

tracks.name: Beware The Blurred Horseman
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. The number of sample free tracks in my repertoire is pretty limited. So I did this one. As mostly my latest is my dearest it kind of fits. Have fun.

intro: In our Featured Artist finale 2015 we'd like to introduce you to Kepz: after the rather naive Trance melodies of his early works he developed a style with a mature depth and a complex sound design such as is rarely to be found on the website. The jury of our Moogfest contest, composed of internationally prestigious musicians and producers, chose him and his Ambient masterpiece 'Lost' as a worthy representative of our highly talented bunch. And they were absolutely right.
about:
Kyryl Polozyuk
Place: Sydney, Australia
Age: 21
Job: IT/Business student at University of Technology Sydney

interview.a: Spacious sound scapes, slowly changing and evolving sounds/atmospheres.
No background.
In 2008 at school we were learning about composing with loops in Cubase and we had our own projects. I really enjoyed it, but I haven't composed anything in my own time until I discovered Audiotool.
Some time in 2009 through Tonematrix on AM lab

Audiotool has been part of my life for the last 6 or so years since I was a little teenager. The community of amazing musicians has had a major influence on my music, what I listen to, artists I discover. The Audiotool team and friends keep inspiring me as a person with their passion and kindness, massive shout-out to Andre Michelle, your dream, vision, what you've achieved and are working on are truly incredible. And of course thanks to others that have put their heart into building parts of Audiotool app and website and growing the community. I'm looking forward to many more years of messing around with the app!

tracks.name: WE TIRE [RIVER EP]
tracks.desc: Aaron Schäfer's skillful use of his guitar combined with creativity and extensive producing knowledge result in very polished, organic and lively sounding music. Clever manipulation of samples gives his tracks that extra depth and complexity.

tracks.name: second guess
tracks.desc: It is hard to believe opaqity's age after you see his 100+ track library of some of the best music on Audiotool. Here, opaqity went in a less expected direction, focusing on the powerful pads, a gentle melody and immersive ambience.

tracks.name: We'll make it through
tracks.desc: It is no surprise even Joel himself had moments of regret since deleting his main page with his work from the last 4+ years. Continuing on a new chapter 'sky bound zoo', he delivers incredibly rich, densely packed and complicated compositions.

tracks.name: rain
tracks.desc: To stick within such well defined boundaries of a genre and to create something so unique, alive, imagery evoking is a work of pure talent. Percussion and noise morphing into the sounds of water, deep echoing chords mimicking distant thunder. A masterpiece.

tracks.name: SURF DUDE
tracks.desc: VLTRA has been perfecting his unique sound over the last couple of years. He has become successful outside of Audiotool, releasing his first record as TLIM SHUG on Echovort Records. His messy lo-fi beats, deep basslines and clever ideas placed him as my favourite artist.

tracks.name: Sandstorm
tracks.desc: Sandburgen was one of the earliest users to show why Audiotool should be taken seriously. 'Sandstorm' has messier and heavier sound design than most of his work, but here it works very well. Planned to be released as part of Sandorama record, it never made the cut being difficult to re-master, becoming a forgotten classic.

tracks.name: kitsch
tracks.desc: Another oldie that is still impressing me almost 4 years later. rnzr is known for pushing the boundaries of Audiotool in the early days. Open up his projects and you'll see a mess of tools and timeline regions that is hard to make sense of.

tracks.name: Realmvision '93
tracks.desc: Created by the same person behind the VLTRA alias, this time bumping the bpm up to 140 with a careful selection of samples. I still can't get enough of this piece and it is without a doubt my favorite track of 2015.

tracks.name: the 30th
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. He produces different kinds of music both with samples and without, but my favorites are his pure ambient tracks. Absence of a beat allows for the imagination and emotions to flow freely.

tracks.name: Lost
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. It's probably the most expected answer, but I'll be honest it is the only track that I wouldn't go back and change. My mind just went with the flow. 'Lost' also turned out to become a ticket to a festival and a once in a lifetime unforgettable experience.

intro: In the two years that he has been with us SharKyyo has worked his way up to become a purposeful deliverer of high quality, darkish Downbeat tracks. He is one of the most passionate supporters of the Audiotool collaboration spirit, as can be seen from the large number of top-class team works he has been part of. It will be interesting to see where the road takes him in the future. We wish him all the best for his recent birthday and a productive new year on Audiotool.
about:
Lyonnel Combes
Place: Lille, north of France near the Belgian border, but born in Lyon.
Age: 41 while writing, 42 when it gets published... or 24, I don't remember...
Job: Sales manager in a technical textiles company (tried to escape this business world for a while but my banker called me back...)

interview.a: Hum, that is a f ... good question ... Do I have a style? Not sure. In fact, I just follow my inspiration, depending on what I listen to at the moment, and my mood. If you guys have a word to define my style, feel free to tell me :) ! Generally speaking I listen to all kinds of music, ranging from Rock (my roots), Pop, Reggae, Stoner, Electro, Folk, World, Trip-Hop to House, Garage, Metal (R.I.P. Lemmy...), Dub etc ... I guess my productions are a mix of all of that!

I played the trumpet for 5 years, drums, learned a bit of keyboard on my own, djembe, had two guitars but never took the time to even try to learn (still one standing in my room ...) I was in a Rock/Metal band in my college years as a drummer, but we never had a chance to really make something out of it. When I started high school, with sport, studying, a job to pay studies and music, I had to make a choice to save some time: that was music ... This has always been a real pain and something I regret. I still wonder how it could have turned out if I had made the choice to go on with music and try to make a living out of it ... Hey, I will never know :)

As far as I remember, I always made noise with any kind of item I was able to grab! As a child I loved trying to reproduce on a keyboard the songs I heard on the radio. My first experience with electronic music was with Jean-Michel Jarre: he made a giant open-air gig in Lyon in 1986 (more than 800 000 people!!) and that was a blast! From then, I started making some "music" (awful 8 bit sounds) on my old Amstrad CPC 464 and my Amiga. Some rocky years later, I discovered 2 guys, Thomas and Guyman, during the 1991/92 new year's evening at a friend's in Paris, making some awesome sounds by mixing House/Techno with groovy Rocky/Disco basic stuffs and that was a second blast! I followed them for a while, from rave to gigs and parties, before I had to move back to Lyon for the Highschool in 1993. What I'm telling you about is the birth of Daft Punk, just before they signed up with Soma records.

At the end of my studies, I discovered "Music", a videogame on Playstation that allowed you to mix 8 tracks of different one shot samples and beats, and I played it for hours!! (years?). That was my last experience with making music for 10 years. When one of my children started to play sax, added to the fact that I quit my job, the vibe came back and I looked for something I could play with on the web, remembering the "Music" game. And then I found Audiotool, I was instantly hooked, that was 2 years ago. I'm still here, despite a lack of time, and I can't imagine doing without it now. I made good friends here, that are also amazing producers! So, here is the occasion to thank the creators of this powerful app that made it possible: 1000 thanks guys!

Just follow your taste and inspiration, don't make music for being followed, make it for yourself. Of course, being listened to and liked by others is cool, but don't try to be anyone else: be yourself. Both inside and outside AT!

tracks.name: Sunshine unplugged
tracks.desc: I could have chosen any track of Sandy's. This one could easily have stood on a Massive Attack album: great guitar, awesome drums, wonderful mood! Maybe my own favorite of his, or at least the one I listened to most often!

tracks.name: Emancipation
tracks.desc: cihangir makes the best Deep House in the place, for me at least. Smooth synths with cool vox and great percs, with delicately slight fx for a cozy atmosphere, taking your mind far far away.

tracks.name: High and Dry
tracks.desc: This track is a good example of the versatility of Floydpjasper. One of my favorite 'Hip Hop' tracks on AT: great percs, so west coast! The first time I heard it, I can well imagine myself saying: where is Snoop?? :)

tracks.name: datura
tracks.desc: He is the man I have made most collabs with. He is so skilled, always hanging around with his crushers. This track is just haunting: deep bass, great production, and cool sharp synths and fx make it a jewel!

tracks.name: Inner demons
tracks.desc: Another one of my buddies here, that has become a real great dark Drum and Bass producer in such a short time. This one comes straight from hell and it's a massive gift made to demons! :)

tracks.name: touch e
tracks.desc: Another talented producer, with a wide range of styles. This is a fast ride on the 303 highway. A bit repetitive? Yes maybe, but so gooood!!!

tracks.name: Acid Burst
tracks.desc: This guy always makes great tracks with crystal clear production! A real master of the 303 machine here on AT.

tracks.name: Dlaboki Vir (Deep Pool)
tracks.desc: Slopo always amazes me with his pure dark Tribal Techno tracks, creating sounds coming from nowhere and everywhere at once, and deeply going through your mind. Beware the bee! :)

tracks.name: Pareidolia
tracks.desc: Horsy has a huge talent for producing big atmosphere tracks that could easily score any blockbuster movie. Always having this 'Chemical Brothers' touch with perfect production!

tracks.name: There Is A Price
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. Really underrated, he produces some of the best bouncing Techno I have ever heard. Discreet and unadvertised, sharp beat with deep bass and groovy rhythm. Always a dark atmosphere.

tracks.name: Last Light Jaws Edit
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. It gives a pretty good idea of me: I'm kind of a geek (track based on a videogame riff), I love it dark, with a Rock/Trip Hop style. I made it in one take as it was flowing and I like it as it is: very rare :).

intro: Aron was quiet rightly placed first in the Drum and Bass section in the "Audiotool Day 2015" competition. With his perfectly produced, chilled D&B contribution he played his way into the hearts of jury and audience alike. You can tell from his tracks that he has invested a lot of time and work in his musical growth. We keep our fingers crossed for him and are pretty sure that this ambitious enthusiasm will take him a long way.
about:
Aron Büch
Place: Veghel, The Netherlands
Age: 17
Job: Right now I don't really have a job or going to school. I finished high school but I don't know what to do after it, so for now I'm just making a load of music. At 17 I still have plenty of time to decide what I want to do anyway.

interview.a: My style is pretty diverse I'd say. Most of the time I make Drum and Bass nowadays, but I also have the Untrust project where I make Chillout stuff, and I still do Dubstep, Trap, whatever occasionally.

My musical background isn't that special. I played the keyboard for a year because my parents wanted me to, but I didn't really learn anything there. I listened to typical lame pop music for a long time. At some point I heard Armin van Buuren's "A State of Trance" show, which had an Arty track that was amazing, so I got into Trance after that. When I went to a new school, there was a kid who said he listened to Skrillex, so I was curious and looked it up. I hated it at first, thought it was just some noise, but after a while I started digging it. Then I looked up all sorts of Dubstep mixes, so I was really into the oldschool sounds of Doctor P, Funtcase, Nero, Skream, Rusko, all of the classic Dubstep kings. My interest in Ambient music was entirely triggered off by amoeba, and my interest in Drum and Bass by CGMan!
I started making music around late 2012, early 2013, on Audiotool.

There was a music teacher at my school in 2010 or something, who made a page on the school website that had some online music programs. Audiotool was one of them. I forgot about it, until I started listening to Skrillex and thought "I can do that too". I got on Audiotool, dragged some samples in, and was very happy with my creation. I remember telling my little brother "oh you fool, why should I use synths if I can just drag in samples…". That was until amoeba commented "try using the synths!". I started doing it, and he'd come back to every track I made, giving amazing advice and telling me that I had talent. I'm incredibly grateful for that.

I'd like to tell the community that the biggest thing in music is putting in the hours. I mean that in multiple ways. First off, I have spoken to a lot of very big artists, and I haven't met a single one that did not spend 4 years before they got anywhere. Don't be sad because you're not the biggest artist on Audiotool or something like that. Most of those people are also veterans. Besides, not everybody who gets stuff done outside of Audiotool is big on Audiotool as well. Just practice hard, and you'll get there.

Secondly, putting in the hours means that your track is not done when you think it's done. When you check out tracks from really good artists, you will notice that there's a lot of processing and tweaking going on. The strong part about good music isn't necessarily good melodies, or good notes, but good sounds. When something sounds good, it doesn't have to be so complicated to make it work. Kepz is a good example. Work on the quality of your sounds. Make every single element sound perfect.

tracks.name: please
tracks.desc: Easy one to choose. This guy is just INSANE. One of the most talented people I've seen on Audiotool and beyond. Never had an emotional connection with a track as strong as I have with amoeba's tracks.

tracks.name: Plane
tracks.desc: Shoutout to my friends at Untrust, this one was really a team effort. We have plenty of finished/nearly finished material, so keep watching us in 2016! We're desperate for Next though.

tracks.name: Interlude I
tracks.desc: This has secretly always been one of my favourite soundscapes on Audiotool. The pad is amazing, and the little clicks are a synth!

tracks.name: Ømega
tracks.desc: A no-brainer. Just open it up, look at the synthwork, and kneel in awe to the C-god.

tracks.name: Underground Lake
tracks.desc: Kepz somehow makes his reverb sound different, and WAY better than everybody elses reverb. The drums and melody are also very creative.

tracks.name: Corner Shop
tracks.desc: I listened to it, was like 'hey, nice track, sick bass, nice kick, cool sidechained noise'. After a while I saw something about the setup in the comment section, so I opened it up, and I literally could not believe my eyes. It doesn't get more impressive than this.

tracks.name: D u n k e l
tracks.desc: Aaron makes such innovative beats. Amazing artist, amazing textures. Very unique style!

tracks.name: Ripples (Luxior chill edit)
tracks.desc: Luxior has some sort of raw talent that I haven't seen anywhere else. Melodically super cool, drum-work always on point, and how the heck did he make the chords at 3:52 sound like that?!

tracks.name: abs x lux - peripherals
tracks.desc: Shoutout to my boy Abstract, his melodies are insane, and the variation this tune in particular has is just incredible!

tracks.name: No Future (ft. CGMan)
tracks.desc: Favorite own track. I started this draft, invited CGMan, we changed some stuff, and then CGMan gave it a proper mixing and compressing session and wow, that made the entire tune. Got some love from Funtcase and a couple of record labels too.

about: Also known as Divus. German producer.

tracks.name: Blue Days feat. Emma
tracks.desc: His tracks have always had a special impact on me and 'Blue Days feat Emma' is probably the track that I have heard most on Audiotool. The Sunday groove with a good beat memorable vocal. Even my friends have it on their playlist.

tracks.name: Memory Eraser
tracks.desc: One of the first tracks on Audiotool that inspired me to go on trying. In some places it reminds me of the music of Booka Shade who often manage to create some kind of blend of dream and reality.

tracks.name: The Dude
tracks.desc: Because he is the dude! the original hipster.

tracks.name: EVERYDAY ANYONE
tracks.desc: The deep-reaching bassline mixed with the beat and the vocals make this track to one of the most mind-blowing pieces on Audiotool. For me Nominal's music is always an incentive for new tracks.

tracks.name: flight _ missing (new edit)
tracks.desc: I can't exactly say what it is about this track that fascinates me so much. I think it's the lightness of it. It simply sounds like a good mood and the main thing.

tracks.name: A Walk In The Dark
tracks.desc: So clear and clean down to the last detail. I am always amazed how he does it. Close your eyes and you will succumb to the magic of it.

tracks.name: KTN-24
tracks.desc: Pushing things forward! The right thing when you are sitting in the tram on the way home. Sometimes it's exactly what you need get your head clear.

tracks.name: Je rave - feat. Twilatoo (pre)
tracks.desc: A favorite of mine with vocals, which is always an inspiration for me. Besides you can always tell if it's an original Sandbrgn. For me he is one of the few artists who have managed to find his own personal style.

tracks.name: Zeitgeist
tracks.desc: The darkest good mood track I know. One of those tracks that wants to make you want to press the App button - you can't resist it.

tracks.name: the man who smokes and is lost
tracks.desc: Artist recommendation. I was pleasantly surprised when I discovered ivs. I am always happy when he releases something new. Especially his short cuts are often a huge inspiration to me and whet my appetite for more.

intro: DubLion began his Audiotool career when he was about twelve years old; at that time he tried his hand - sometimes more, sometimes less successfully – at crunchy Dubstep and Drum and Bass. Four years and some hours of practice later he produces optimally mixed Drum and Bass. Here his particularly strong point is the unintrusive instrumentation, so that despite driving beats we can be sure of a goodly portion of „chill". Yet another Audiotool youngster we should definitely keep an eye on.
about:
Thomas Waller
Place: Oxfordshire, England
Age: 15 and 8 months
School/Job: I am currently in year 11 which is the last year of secondary school in the UK. I will hopefully start College this September and I will be doing a course called Music technology.

interview.a: My style is very chilled Drum & Bass. I make chilled Garage songs when I am in the mood.

I started getting the vibe for Drum and Bass when I was around 11 years old. I started listening to lots of Drum and Bass artists on YouTube at that time. I've always been listening to YouTube channels such as UKF, Hospital Records, Liquicity and MED School Records for inspiration. I started building a collection of Drum and Bass cds when I was 13 years old and it's become quite a large hobby since. I have never learnt to play instruments but was interested in guitar from around the ages of 7 to 9 years old.

At the end of 2012 I wanted to begin making my own music. At the time I wanted a free music DAW to start me off and build on my skills of music production. It took me almost 2 years to find my style of Drum and Bass that just clicked with me. Ever since I have been pushing myself to create more sophisticated Drum and Bass. My friend started around the same time as me. One of them was FlexScape. He gave up making music because he didn't give the time and effort to it and he didn't believe in himself, that he could get any better.

Just keep pushing your boundaries and find the music that connects with you. Use the artists that inspire you to create your own style of music which is also similar to theirs at the same time. With Audiotool you have to take your time and experiment with different things. What works and what doesn't work. If you think your music sounds good you have to work until its 110 percent perfection. Control each element in your song to the way you want it to sound. My 2016 coming album will be a good example of that so watch out. :)

tracks.name: Kodama(Ft. Mourn)
tracks.desc: This track gives me some Ambient vibes. The solid drums and clear bassline show his originality. Notoz always delivers his best and is someone who always works to perfection.

tracks.name: ides of march
tracks.desc: There are no words to describe how beautiful this song is. Tornsage uses a steady beat and combines vocals and pianos to create the perfect mix.

tracks.name: Focus
tracks.desc: Mikke created such a soul-touching song with this one. This inspired me to progress further when collaborating with Notoz.

tracks.name: Distant.
